origin_explanation: for G. gə, see Conti Rossini 1912:193; Arg. gə, Amh. gə (Getatchew 1970:78); in Amh. also in bälä-gə rude' (lit. 'a man of the countryside'); with place-names as in harär-gə, sälam-gə, gurage-gə, and in combinations such as, 'head of the bed': Amh. ras-gə, Gur. gwənnän-gə, dumi-gə; 'at the foot of': Amh. gar-gə, əgər-gə (see Praetorius 1879:406; Cohen 1939:100). Cohen GLECS 1 (1933). 34 connects gə with Heb. gay 'valley', Ar. ğiwā' 'valley' (rather than ğaww 'interior').
undoubtedly wrongly copied by MA instead of əgəzaza (see gəz II).

meaning: 'loathe, revile, disparage, chide, blame, upbraid, reproach, (K) be irritated, be annoyed'
'quarrel, strive, dispute with one another, have a dispute, vie, contend, object, argue, speak ill of'
'quarrel, quibble, litigation, reviling, contention, strife, controversy, dispute, discussion, disagreement, reprimand'
'contentious, who stimulates contention, litigious, wrangling, disputer, argumentative'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tā)gəzəzə 'quarrel' (v.), gəzəz 'quarrel' (n.), Amh. gəz 'dispute, argument', gəzəzən 'reproach, blame'; perhaps also Cushitic: Bil. gūs 'reject, hate' (Reinisch 1887:165).

SEMITIC: Soq. goihe 'be ripe' (perhaps 'be half-ripe'). ETHIOPIC: Tna. gə'ä'e 'be unripe', gə'ə 'unripe fruit', gə'a 'cotton boll that is ready to burst open'; also in Cushitic: Bil.Qua. gī 'become ripe', Qem. gi.
for əgələt see gal.

meaning: 'cry out, cry in a loud voice, clamor, groan, moan, sigh, wail, lament'
origin_explanation: mann 1186. Barth 1893:18, as against Fraenkel 1898:7). Heb. gā'ar 'roar', Aram.-Syr. gə'ar 'shout at', Ug. gʻr 'roar, rebuke'. The root is related to Ar. ğahura 'be loud (voice)', Md. ghr 'vibrate'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. gä'arä 'shout', Te. gä'arä 'shout', Amh. garä 'cry out', gʷəggʷärä 'bellow out' (reduplicative gʷər); related to (a)gʷärä 'gʷər') 'roar, bellow'. Har. girgir bäya 'be noisy'. (a)gʷärra 'bellow'.

meaning: 'cry, shout, clamor, outcry, groan, groaning, lamentation, wailing, complaint'
'who cries out, who groans, who laments'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Syria) ğarar 'low, moo'. Also Ar. ğarara (Dozy), related to class. Ar. ğarara (Dillmann 1186. Barth 1893:18, as against Fraenkel 1898:7). Heb. gā'ar 'roar', Aram.-Syr. gə'ar 'shout at', Ug. gʻr 'roar, rebuke'. The root is related to Ar. ğahura 'be loud (voice)', Md. ghr 'vibrate'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. gä'arä 'shout', Te. gä'arä 'shout', Amh. garä 'cry out', gʷəggʷärä 'bellow out' (reduplicative gʷər); related to (a)gʷärä 'gʷər') 'roar, bellow'. Har. girgir bäya 'be noisy'. (a)gʷärra 'bellow'.

meaning: 'give to drink, put food into someone's mouth, (Y.K) force a baby to drink, (T.K.M) prepare porridge'

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. (a)gʷä'ata 'prepare porridge', gʷə'atä, Amh. gʷatä 'force a baby or an animal to drink'; Te.Tna. gä'at 'porridge', Amh. gʷat. The verb is perhaps a denominative from *gʷa'at, from 'feed with milk', below.
possibly reconstructed from Amh. gʷət 'width of the palm across the knuckles'.
from Amh. gʷət, same.

meaning: 'deceived, deluded, (Lt) overcome by work, (T) weak, that falls into ruin'

'deceit, deception, a ment of a spirit, illusion, (K) fortune-hard labor, toil, fatigue, (Lt) deterioration, decay'. While 'hard labor' is not given by Dillmann, several examples quoted have the meaning of 'hard labor, toil'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. (tə)gʷä'ata 'be distressed', (a)gʷə'atäyä 'despise, scorn', Amh. gʷat 'work, toil' (from Geez). Dillmann 1186 derives gʷə'əyä 'deception' from Gk. goēteia 'magic, art of casting spells'. One should perhaps consider the possibility of a root gʷə'əy I 'deceive, fool', and gʷə'əy II 'be overcome by hard work, become weak'.

meaning: 'move, change camp, change residence, depart, encamp, pitch camp, march (into), go on a trip'
'transfer, transport, move (tr.), remove, make wander, send into exile, banish'
'baggage (of an army), caravan that follows an army (family, animals, equipment)'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. ğāza (ğwz) 'pass along', ğāza 'cross, go over', from ğəz 'cut' (Dillmann 1187; Brockelmann 1908:273), Heb. gāz (gwz) 'pass along'. ETHIOPIC: Te. gäzäzə 'emigrate, depart with one's family', Tna. gäzäzə 'depart', gäzəz, gʷəzəz 'baggage', Amh. (tä)gʷäzäzə 'travel, go on a trip', (a)gəzəz 'banish', gʷəzo 'baggage'. Har. gäzəz 'change residence'. Gur. gəzəzə; Amh.Gur. gəzəz 'caravan'. Har. gäz. Reinisch 1887:134 compares Bil. gä'd 'move'. Sa. gedi 'move to another pasture' (with alternance z:d).

meaning: 'become free, be set free, be emancipated'
'set free, make free, send forth free (a slave)'
'become free, be made free, be set at liberty, free oneself'
'free, freeman, one who is set free'

'nature, manner, custom, behavior, conduct, fashion, character, habit(s), disposition, temperament, essence, quality, mode of life, ways; the Geez language, the first order of the Ethiopian syllabary, one of the three modes of plainchant' (Velat 75, 625ff; for other modes, see uräräy, əzl).

'freedom, manumission, liberation'; gərzannā 'liberation from slavery, status of freedom'; agəraz agərazi 'freeman, free man, master, Ethiopian'; agərazit 'free woman'; agərazo agərazō (Lt) 'manumission', lit. 'setting free of a slave'; agərazay (Lt) agərazāy 'free, freeman'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. guzun 'Ethiopian' (W. Müller). ETHIOPIC: For Tigre and Tigrinya only a few meanings are registered in the existing dictionaries, but they undoubtedly have the various meanings of Geez. Tna. gə`əz` 'habit, the Geez language, first order of the Ethiopian syllabary, one of the three modes of the plainchant'; Te. agəza 'spell', agə`azi` 'clever'; Amh. (a)gəzā 'liberate', gə`əz` 'habit, ac- tion(s), character, the Geez language, the first order of the Ethiopian syllabary, one of the three modes of the plainchant', gə`əzan` 'freedom', gə`əzanna` 'liberation from slavery'. Several meanings of Tna. gə`əz`, and Amh. gə`əz` are undoubtedly borrowed from Geez.

SEMITIC: Ar. gubb 'well, pit' (Jeffery 98 considers it an Aramaic loanword since it is used in the Koran in the Joseph story; however, gubb is used in all Arabia according to Landberg 19:259). Heb. gēb 'pit, ditch'. Aram.-Syr. gubbā, gubbā, Md. guba, Ug. gb (Caquot 313), Akk. gubbu 'water well'. While Zimmern 44 (followed by Brockelmann 1928: 100) considers Akk. gubbu to be the origin of the Semitic root, von Soden 295 considers Akk. gubbu an Aramaic loanword. See also Rundgren, OS 10 (1961).107. ETHIOPIC: Te. gabb 'cave, hole, pit'. Amh. gabb, Har. gāb.

meaning: 'return, leave, turn back, come back, enter, be delivered, be restored'; gabā labbu 'he was in his right mind, he became conscious'); gabā krəstənnā [Lt] 'be rechristened'; combined with another verb it means 'again'; e.g. wagabā wala aka 'he sent me again', gabā a, same as astagābā a; agəbā a 'turn (tr.), make return, bring, bring back, take back, receive, hand over, restore, restitute, revoke, put up, set recompense, recover, give over, betray'; (zayagābba a 'traitor'); tagābā a 'be turned back, be gathered, turn (intr.)'; tagābā a 'be collected, be compiled, be gath- ered, gather together (intr), assemble (intr), be assembled, be united with one another, be circumscribed, come together, come to an agreement'; astagābā a (astagəb a) 'gather, collect, bring into, assemble, compile, cause to be gathered, call up (spirits), bring together, recover, heap up, restore, sum up, bring back, take up, cause (a city) to surrender, take by force of arms'; gabā a 'i. act. part. of gab a; also 'hired servant, mercenary'; gabu wubu 'who returns, assembled, gath- ered'; gabā e (Lt) 'cave'; gubā e, gəbā e 'gathering, assembly, reun- ion, council, congregation, synod, session, meet- ing, community (of faithful), collection'; gubā e qānā 'kind of qənē-poem'; Velat 64; gubā eyāwi 'pertaining to gathering'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. gaba a 'turn back, remain behind, be- stain (with 'an 'from'), come upon' (with 'ala), also without alā; (see Dillmann 1168; F. Rund- gren, OS 10 [1961]. 105ff "gaba a 'sich zurückzie- hen, zurückkehren' kann an sich als eine Art vox media betrachtet werden"). Sar. gb 'return back', h-gb 'transfer'. One wonders whether the various meanings of the root gb belong to a single root or whether one should not consider gb I 'return' and gb II 'collect'. Hebrew gebe e (1) 'cistern' by Ludolf 659 (followed by Rundgren, OS 10 [1961].107; Ullendorff 192) with G. gəb would then be derived from gb II 'collect'. For gb I the root is related to Ar. gābā (gby). Aram.-Syr. gābā, Md. gāba. ETHIOPIC: Tna. gāb e 'turn, return, pass'. Te. gāb a 'came gā a happen', see Cohen 1924:131). Amh. gābā 'enter, return home, live, reside', Gaf. gābā 'return home, live, reside', and also Har. girāgābā 'return', Gur. gurāgābā 'return', gābā 'to go back to' *Gbg (with dissimilation of g-gb to g-gb which represents a reduplicated root of gb. For 'gathering', cp. Te.Tna.Amh. gubave.
ETHIOPIC: Te.Amh. gābā, Tna. gaba. For gābā, see Strelcyn 1973:no.100; from Cushitic: Be. gāba, Bil. gūbā.

meaning: 'side, flank, rib, loins'; (sab a gabawāt, əllə əmgabawāt 'collaterals'); (arāftə a gabo 'lateral wall'); gabawā (MA) 'be at the side of, be close to' (denominative);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: The Semitic *gab (b) derives from gnb, gənb, to judge from Ar. ganb 'side' (Dillmann 1173), Md. gamba, ganba. The root gnb became gabb in PBh. *gabbe, in lə-gabbe 'near to, concerning', Aram.- Syr. gabbā 'side'. Akk. gabbu 'part of human or animal body'. ETHIOPIC: The Ethiopic *gab is probably independ- ent of *gnb of the other Semitic languages. Thus, Te. gabo 'side', Tna. gobo; also in Cushitic: Bil.Qua. gābā 'side', Kham. gebā. See also Cohen, BSL 21 (1928-9).136-7, and Brockelmann 1950:21.

meaning: 'pit, ditch, cavern, abyss, cave, lair, hole, den';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. gubb 'well, pit' (Jeffery 98 considers it an Aramaic loanword since it is used in the Koran in the Joseph story; however, gubb is used in all Arabia according to Landberg 19:259). Heb. gēb 'pit, ditch'. Aram.-Syr. gubbā, gubbā, Md. guba, Ug. gb (Caquot 313), Akk. gubbu 'water well'. While Zimmern 44 (followed by Brockelmann 1928: 100) considers Akk. gubbu to be the origin of the Semitic root, von Soden 295 considers Akk. gubbu an Aramaic loanword. See also Rundgren, OS 10 (1961).107. ETHIOPIC: Te. gabb 'cave, hole, pit'. Amh. gabb, Har. gāb.

meaning: 'entering, return, military inspection, conversion, betrayal'; (gab at maret 'burial', lit. 'entering the earth'); (gab at manqolāt 'withdrawal of the curtain'); agəbā i 'traitor'; agəbə ot 'restitution'; astagābā i 'convener'; astagābā i 'who gathers, who compiles, (Gr.337) magician, sorcerer'; astagəbu astagəbu 'collected, collection, ered together'; (masḥafā astagəbu 'collected writings'); magəbā 'returning, return, recurring, gathering place, refuge'; magubā (K*) mag bā 'confluence, place where rivers flow together, (K) place of gathering'; mastagābā mastagābā i 'one who assembles, one who gathers, collection' (Velat 219ff); mastagə bā (K*), see mag bā; mastagəbu mastagəbu 'gathered, collected, ing place, place of gathering, place of contract- ing (shrinking)'; mastagubā mastagubā e 'assembly, con- tion, meeting place'; tagābə o (t) 'synod, council, congregation, lection'.
origin_explanation: gabbā a market is derived by Praetorius 1879:171 from gb and would mean originally 'gathering'. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. gaba a 'turn back'. See also gabbab, below.

SEMITIC: Ar. gabbāb 'plentiful, copious, numerous', Ar. (Dat.) gabbab 'gather here and there'. PBh. gabbāb 'gather', Aram. gabbab, Akk. gabbu 'totality, the whole, entirety', related to Ar. gābā (gby) 'gather, collect'. Aram.-Syr. gabbā (gby), Md. gāba. ETHIOPIC: Te. gābab 'troop'. DTW 217 translates Amh. gābāggābā 'gather food', this sense coming from the original meaning 'desire, be stingy'.

SEMITIC: Heb. gab in gabbe- 'bosses on a shield'. PBh. gab 'back, hunch' (Cohen 1947:no.202), Syr. gābībā 'hunch-backed', Md. gāb (gbb) 'bend, curve'; for Ar. of South Arabia, see Landberg 1909:1643-4. ETHIOPIC: Amh. g əbbābā, g əbbābā alā 'be bent'. Te. gābb bela 'incline', Tna. g ag bā 'be crooked, be twisted' (from g ə-g b-b); also in Cushitic: Af. gūb 'be bent'.

meaning: '(T) gabgābo 'entrance, reception room, vestibule, open area in the middle of a room, (T.KG) assembly, council held in front of the entrance';
origin_explanation: perhaps from gb (see gab a) 'enter, gather' > gb > gb-gb (reduplicative gb). While the derivation from wgb is possible (cp. habhab 'gift', from whb), the connection with Ar. waqaba 'in die Höhle eintreten' (Mh. weqāb 'enter') suggested by Bittner, WZKM 27 (1913).129 is unlikely.

SEMITIC: W. Müller suggests Ar. waqaba 'be obliga- tory, be binding', G. gəbgāb coming from gb-gb, from wgb. ETHIOPIC: the comparison with Amh. gābāgāb 'stin- gy, greedy' suggested by Noeldeke, WZKM 19 (1905). 406 is not convincing; probably common with Cushitic: Bil. gab 'oppress'.
SEMITIC: Soq. gībeleḥ 'hollow place in which there is water'.

meaning: 'water basin near a cistern for animals'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC. Te. gäbla 'water basin near a cistern for animals'. Tna. gabla 'trough'. Amh. gabla 'place where cattle spend the night' (would it be near the trough?).

meaning: (T) 'place from which a proclamation is read'; perhaps refers to a proper name.

origin_explanation: from Ar. ǧubn 'cheese'; also Amh. gäbna. In the other Semitic languages: Mh. ǧiben, Heb. gəbīnā, Syr. gəbeṭṭā (from gəbaṭṭā), Aram. ǧubnā, Akk. ǧubnatu. For an Akkadian form gabān, see H. Huffner, JAOS 86 (1966).27-33. As for the origin of the noun, Koehler 167 and Baumgartner 167 (with a question mark) derive it from the root gbn 'be hunched' whereas Buhl 127 derives it from gbn II 'curdle' and separates it from gbn I 'be hunched'.

meaning: 'act, do, work, make, be active, practice, labor, perform, manufacture, produce, bring forth, create, build, fashion, function, carry out, prepare, achieve, execute, procure, enact, keep (ordinances), observe (ordinances, fast)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC. Ar. ǧabara 'force to do something, ǧabara 'powerful man, constraint, coercion', ǧibbor 'manly', Aram.-Syr. ǧabbar 'strong, prevail', Ph. gbr-t 'deeds', Akk. gapāru 'surpass', gabru, gapru. For the verb 'make' used in the express magical activity, cp. Aram. ʾbd, Heb. maʿaśe (Strelcyn 1955:XXI-XXII). ETHIOPIC. Te. gäbra 'do, make', gäbbära 'subdue', Tna. gäbärä 'make, do', Amh. 'farm', agäbbärä 'force', Har. agäbära 'tame', gäbbärä. For Geez gäbr 'taxes', cp. Tna. gäbbärä 'pay taxes', Te.Arg. gäbbära, Gur. Amh.Te.Gur. gäbär 'taxes', Tna.Har. gäbr. The root passed into Cushitic: Kam. gäbbärä, Tem. gäbbärä, Sa. gibr-e 'work', Had. gi 'pay taxes', Kam. gibirro.
ETHIOPIC. Amh. gäbrodähi 'area around a compound'. Note the ending -ähi in gabrondähi this ending see Guidi 1906:921.

ETHIOPIC. Amh. gäbronähi. DTW 224 identifies -hi with -w and translates it 'their slave'. Note also -ähi in gabrodähi, and gandorähi. It is strange that gabronähi has the same meaning as gandorähi, below.
would it mean 'barley-colored' from gäbs? for a similar description, cp. Amh. gäbəṣma dəro 'chicken that has small white spots on its wing feathers'.

from Gk. gypsos 'plaster'; also in Ar. ǧibs 'plaster', ǧibsīn (Dozy), Syr. ǧupśīn, ǧubśīn 'lime', Aram. ǧipśos, ǧipśis, gəbśis, PbH. gəbeṣ 'lime, plaster', gippeṣ 'plaster' (v). For Ar. ǧiṣṣ (< ǧibs, ǧibs) 'lime', ǧiṣṣ of the same origin, see Fraenkel 9-10; Vollers, ZDMG 51 (1897). 296. Von Soden 282 derives Ar. ǧaṣṣ (ǧiṣṣ), Aram.-Syr. ǧaṣṣā from Akk. ǧaṣṣu. For Gk. gypsos coming from Semitic, see O. Blau, ZDMG 25 (1871).542. See also gamśas I.

meaning: (M) wooden cover of a parchment manuscript';
origin_explanation: W. Müller suggests, with reservations, Gk. gabathon, gabatha 'bowl, dish'. For a possible Semitic origin of Gk. gabathon, see Masson 75. ETHIOPIC. Te. gäbäta 'measure of corn', Amh. Arg. gäbäta 'large wooden bowl, measure of grain', Gur. gäbäta 'plate made of wood', Har. gäbäta 'eating bowl'. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Or. gabata 'table, plate' is considered by E. Haberland, Galla Süd-Äthiopiens, p. 422 an Amharic loanword; also Had. gabata 'plate', Kam. gabati-ta.
probably to be identified with gabata I.
From Amh. gäbäta 'high hill'; also in Cushitic: Qem. gubätta 'hill'.
from Amh. gäbät.
variant of gäfṭä'a (see below).

meaning: ‘hit, strike’; gabt, gabat, gəb! ‘colic, stomach ache, nausea, hookworm, (T.OT.588) rumbling of the bowels caused by dysentery’; tagəbt (K*) ‘diarrhea, dysentery’
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. gäbbäṭä ‘hit, whack’, gäb! ‘upset stomach’, perhaps Tna. gäbbäṭä ‘knead’, that is, ‘strike the dough’. For ‘stomach ache’ from ‘cut, incise’, see qaraṣa. See also gabaṭbäṭ.

meaning: ‘a game using beans or pebbles played on a board’;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh.Gur. gäbäṭa, Te. gäbäṭä; also in Cushitic: Al. gäbäṭa, Mo. gabato. For the gabäṭä-game, see R. Pankhurst, Ethiopia Observer 14 (1971). 154-206; Azania 17 (1982).27-42.
from Amh. gabṭo, same.
from gbt with reduplication of the last two radicals; see gabata, above.
SEMITIC: Ar. ğabaza ‘cut, set aside’. ETHIOPIC: Amh. gäbbäzä ‘be a riverbank, be a boundary’. See also gabaz II.

meaning: ‘watch, keeper, overseer, admin-istrator of a church, person who manages the internal affairs of the church, sacristan’; qaysä gabaz, qasisa gabaz ‘administrator of a church’;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. gäbäz ‘administrator of a church’. Dillmann 1174, perhaps rightly, connects gabaz I and gabaz II; in this case the basic meaning would be ‘riverbank’, that is ‘guard, protector’. See also Walker 109-10, Conti Rossini 1923:375.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. gäbbäzä ‘be feeble’.
from Ar. ğādd; also in Te.Amh. gadd ‘luck, for-tune’, Tna. gaddi, Te. gäd; in Cushitic: Had. gadava ‘luck’, Qem. gäz, with alternance d : z.

meaning: ‘cut with an axe, dig a ditch, excavate’; gəḍb (T) guḍb ‘axe’; gäḍḥä (K*) ‘trench, ditch, gully, low place where water collects’;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. ğadaba ‘tear out by pulling’ (developed from the original meaning ‘pull, draw to oneself’). ETHIOPIC: Te. (ta)gäḍäba ‘split into’, gäḍäb ‘ax-Tna. gäḍäbä ‘cut’, Amh. gäḍḍäbä ‘dig, cut’ (al-gəḍḍäbä ‘cut, carve’, with augmented n) mäğäḍäbiya, gəḍäba, gəḍoba ‘axe’; also in Cushitic Bil. guḍub ‘axe’, Som. ğidib. The root is related to gdm (see below) with alternance of labials.

meaning: ‘gift, present, offering, tribute’; gadawa (K*) ‘make a gift’ (denomina-tive);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ğadd ‘gift, bounty’, gädā (ğdw) ‘give a gift’, Sar. gädy-t ‘grant of land’. ETHIOPIC: Amh. gäda ‘gift, present’ (from Geez). The connection with Te. gäda ‘admiration, astonish-ment’ suggested by Littmann-Höfner 598 is doubt-ful.

meaning: ‘force, compel (K) be cruel, be deformed’; gäḍuḍ ‘serious, severe, (T) impure, dirty’; (bagaḍud [Lt] ‘by force’); gadd, gəddat, (T.M) gəḍḍənnat ‘severity, vehe-mence’;
origin_explanation: M. 150 translated gadada by ‘get worse’. A view of the meaning of this root (see gəḍuḍ) seems to have misunderstood the Amharic ba-sä (given by Y). While its basic meaning is ‘be bad, become worse’, it also serves for the in-tensification or seriousness of a condition (such as disease) and it is the meaning ‘be serious’ that is correct for gadada. Indeed, K 300 renders Geez gəḍuḍ by Amh. yä-basä, yä-ṣänna, bä-rätta, expressions implying strength. SEMITIC: Ar. ğadda ‘appear serious’, ğidd ‘zeal’, gəḍuḍ ‘serious’ is not to be derived from Semitic gəḍd ‘cut’, as suggested by Buhl 129 and Brockelmann 1928:103. ETHIOPIC: Te. gəḍḍa ‘compulsion, force’, gäḍḍa (gdd) ‘arra-cher à la fille l'aveu de l'auteur de sa grossesse’. Tna. gäḍädi ‘force, compel’, Amh. (as-)gäḍäda ‘force’, Had. gəḍḍa, Bil. gəḍḍ and gəḍḍä ‘force’.

meaning: ‘piece off with an axe or with a saw’;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ğaddä ‘cut’, Šh. ğədd ‘do something’ (from the original meaning h-gdd ‘validate a decree < decide < cut’ in htgəḍəḍ ‘make incisions in oneself’), Syr. gaḍ (gdd), Md. gəḍḍ ‘cut off, put an end to’. Akk. gaḍādu ‘chop’. ETHIOPIC: Te. gäḍḍa (gdy) ‘tear off’ of meat (severed from the bone)’. Amh. gäḍḍay ‘bo-dy derived from gdl ‘kill’, and not from gdy, as sug-gested in D. Cohen 1976:100.
Brockelmann 1928:104 gives the impression that Geez gəḍuḍ is to be identified with Ar. ğund ‘army’. Heb. gəḍuḍ, but in fact it is a transcription of Te. gäḍo ‘brigand’ as given by D. Cohen 1976:99.

meaning: ‘walk proudly or full of assurance, strut’; g"addä (Amh) ‘g"aḍḍäḍi ‘walk slowly and proudly, be puffed up’.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. (tä)g"aḍḍäḍi ‘walk slowly and proudly, be puffed up’.

meaning: ‘throw away, thrust, re-ject, cast away, discard, cast down, cast aside, retrench, give up, abandon, omit, repudiate, despise, lose, avoid, deduct, forget’; tagadfa, passive; also ‘stray, go astray’; gəḍäfi, act. part. of gadfa; gəḍuf, pass. participle of gadfa; also ‘cast-away, outcast, lost, perverse, that goes astray’; gəḍfat ‘rejection, omission, mistake, error, neg-ligence’;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ğadafa ‘blaspheme, revile’, Heb. giddəp, Aram-Syr. gaddəp, Md. gəḍp ‘scrape, cut away, blaspheme, revile’. For ‘throw > repudiate, blaspheme’, cp. Ar. qadafa ‘throw, defame’. G. wa-daya ‘throw’, ‘astawadaya ‘calumniate’. See also Wagner 39. ETHIOPIC: Te. gäḍfa ‘throw away’. Tna. gäḍäfa ‘abandon, throw away’. Amh. gäḍäffa ‘forget some-thing one has learned, skip a line’, Gur. gäḍäfa ‘break a fast’; passed into Cushitic: Kam. gaddafu-ta ‘food taken after an anthelminitic’, that is, ‘break a fast’, Qem. gäḍäf ‘break a fast’. See also g"aḍf.

SEMITIC: Ar. ğadaf ‘dregs’, Ar. (Yemen) kuḍḍāfa ‘rubbish’. ETHIOPIC: from a root g"ḍf (g"adafa) recorded only by K who considers it an Amharic loanword. The root also occurs in Tna. g"äḍuf ‘rubbish’, Te. gəḍəf, Amh. g"əḍəf ‘cast-out things’. Har. guḍuf ‘place where rubbish is placed’, Gur. guḍuf ‘dirt’. There is also the possibility of identifying it with gadafa, above.
is either wrongly copied by MA instead of gəsfara 7lM (see below) or is borrowed from Amh. g"ä-ḍäffärä.

meaning: g"ad-a (yag"där), g"ad-a (yag"där) 7l-7l ‘strike, smite, thrust, knock, touch, butt, heave with sobs’; tag"ad-a, passive; also ‘be injured’; g"ad-a ‘strike one another, clap one against the other, be entangled, clash’; g"ad-a (Gr) ‘injure violently’; g"ad-a, act. part. of g"ad-a; g"ad-u (K*), pass. part. of g"ad-a; g"ad-at ‘striking, stroke’; mag"da (T) ‘hammer’;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ğada ‘cut off, mutilate’, Heb. gäda ‘cut’, Aram-Syr. gəda ‘amputate’. ETHIOPIC: Te. gäd ‘a push, pound’, Tna. g"äd ‘a ‘crush, damage’, Amh.Arg. g"ädä ‘a ‘harm, damage’, Gur. g"äd ‘a ‘passed into Cushitic: Tem. gədda’e, but Bil. gad ‘smite, hit’ is common with Ethiopic.

meaning: kn°ock (at the door), (T.M) clap hands rhythmically, strike, accompany a dance with hand clapping'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. g°adg°ad bäla 'thunder' (v.), näg°äd-g°ad 'thunder' (n.), Amh. täng°ädägg°ädä 'thunder' (v.), näg°ädg°ad 'thunder' (n.), Gur. näg°ädg°ad 'thunder' (n.). It is from näg°ädg°ad that a second ary verb ng°d developed: Tna. näg°ädä 'thunder' (v.), Amh. nägg°ädä; also in Cushitic: Bil. näg°äd, Kham. nug°ad, Af. angädä (see Dolgopolsky 176).

meaning: strive', tagädala 'wrestle, struggle, strive, contend, fight, battle, combat, fight a spiritual fight', 'astagädäla, causative of tagädäla; gadl 'strife, striving, conflict, contending, combat, mortification, spiritual fight, life (of a saint)'; tagädälo(t) 'conflict, strife, struggle, wres tling'; tagädäli, part. of tagädäla; also 'ascetic'; mastagädäl 'champion, fighter, combatant, wrestler, one who fights a spiritual fight, mar tyr, ascetic'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ğadala 'be strong', Soq. godil, Heb. gädal 'be big'. Ug. gdl 'large'. The Arabic root comes to mean 'to quarrel, fight' in the derived stems such as tagädäla, tagädäla. ETHIOPIC: Te. gadala 'wrestle, fight', gädla 'conflict', gädli 'strife, life of a saint', Tna. tägädäla 'fight', Amh. tägädälä 'struggle', Gur. tägädälä 'endeavor to do something'. This root is probably to be identified with gadala II, but since gadala II in the meaning 'kill' is an Amharism it seemed appropriate to separate the two verbs.

SEMITIC: Ar. ğadala 'small, dwarf' (from 'cut'), relat ed to Ar. ğadama, ğazama 'cut off, section', Soq. ğdm 'be cut', SAR. ğdm(n) 'deliverance from dif ficulty', PbH. ğädam 'cut', Aram.-Syr. ğadam 'cut down', Md. ğdm, Akk. ğadāmu 'cut off hair'; augmented by r in Ar. (Syria) ğardam, PbH. ğirdam, Aram.-Syr. ğardem. ETHIOPIC: Te. gaddom 'pickaxe' (probably not bor rowed from Ar. quddūm, as suggested by Littmann Höfner 599), Amh. gägämo 'axe'; also in Cushitic: gaddumä 'axe', Som. gudūmo. The root is aug mented by r in Te. gärdäma 'break off, break in pieces', Tna. g°ärdämä, Amh. gärdädämä, g°ärdä mä 'break', Gur. g°ärdädämä 'split in half'.

An Amharism for the meaning 'kill' in the Ethio pian languages, cp. Arg. gäddälä, Har. gäddälä, Gur. gäddälä. Praetorius 1879:72 compares gädl 'kill' with gez qtl, but since qtl, qtl also exists in Gurage one would have to conclude that the root has doublets. For the relationship between gdl and qtl, see also Beguinot 529ff.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. g°ädälä 'diminish, decrease, be miss ing', Amh. Gur. g°ädälä, Arg. g°ädälä, Har. g°ädälä; passed into Cushitic: Bil. g°ädäl 'become lean, thin'. See also g°ädäle.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. g°ädälä 'diminish, decrease, be miss ing', Amh. Gur. g°ädälä, Arg. g°ädälä, Har. g°ädälä; passed into Cushitic: Bil. g°ädäl 'become lean, thin'. See also g°ädäle.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: S. Euringer, ZS 6 (1928), 92 connects it with g°ädäle, gudäle 'deficiency, lack' (see g°ädala, above), which developed into 'consumption, an evil spirit that causes consumption'. See also Dobberahn Getatchew Haile, Paideuma 20 (1983), 114, n.14; Mercier 290ff.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. (tä)gadäma 'lie down to sleep', Tna. (tä)gadäma, Amh.Gur. (tä)gadäma; Te. g°adom 'edge, side', Tna. g°ädmi 'side, flank', Amh. g°ädäma 'direction, side', g°ädm 'width (of church house), horizontal road', Gur. g°ädmän 'horizontal'; also in Cushitic: Bil. g°adum 'side', Sa. g°adäm 'stretch out'. Be. gedim 'side'. Note from Dillmann 1202 combines gadama and gadda ma. See also g°admä and mgdm.

ETHIOPIC: Te. g°ädam 'monastery, convent', gädam 'plain, g°ädam 'outside', Tna.Amh. gädam 'convent', Tna. g°ädam 'outside'. The meanings 'outside, plain, monastery' go back to the same root gdm (since monasteries are normally in the wilderness), contra ry to Littmann-Höfner 599 who record for Tigre three different roots: gädam 'convent', gädam 'plain, country', and g°ädam 'outside'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. gafä'a 'bring down an adversary, uproot (a tree)'; related to Ar. gafä'a 'turn upside down, lift someone up and throw him to the ground'. ETHIOPIC: Te. gafä'a 'push away, treat unjustly', Tna. gafä'a, Amh. arg. gafä'a 'push, shove, oppress', Har. gofä'a, Gofa, Gur. gäfä'a; passed into Cushitic: Bil. gafa 'offend'.

phonetics: gff, gafäfä (yagfäf) ገፈፈ (K*) 'strip off', gafäfäfä (Lt) 'booty, pillage'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Perhaps Ar. gafäfa (VIII) 'empty a receptacle'. ETHIOPIC: Te. gäfäfa (gff) 'take away everything, rob'. Tna. gäfäfä 'plunder'. Amh. gäfäfäfä 'strip off, pillage'; passed into Cushitic: Bil.Sa. gafaf 'plunder, strip'.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. gafhe 'be widened', Te. gufəh 'large'. Amh. gäfäfa 'grow' mentioned by Littmann-Höfner 604 comes from gfə and does not belong here; it means 'push' and only with ədmə 'age' does it mean 'grow, get on in years'. cp. rather Amh. gäfäfa 'protrude, bulge (stomach)', possibly from gfəh.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. gäfäṭä.

meaning: 'destroy utterly, pervert, subvert, cast down, overturn, throw down, upset, turn away (from)'; tagafṭə'a, passive; gafṭa'i, act. part. of gafṭə'a; gafṭu'ə, pass. part. of gafṭə'a; also 'perverse, crooked'; gafṭa'ə 'subversion, overthrowing'; gafṭə'awi 'subversion, overthrowing'.

For the various Arabic dialects, see Leslau 1938:114. ETHIOPIC: Te. gäfṭə'a 'put on top, turn over, upset'. Tna. gäfṭə'ə. See also gabṭə'a, above.

ETHIOPIC: Amh. gagano; probably onomatopoeic.

meaning: 'hurry, rush, do rashly, be quick, hasten, carry out hastily, flee, beat fast (heart), be in a state of trepidation, be restless, be assiduous, be zealous, be solicitous, be thoughtful'; ag°agg°ä'a, causative; also 'press, hasten, urge to confound, disturb, agitate'; tag°agg°ä'a 'be alarmed, (Gr) haste'; g°agg°ä'i, act. part. of g°agg°ä'a; g°äggu, g°ägg°ə 'hurrying, speedy, hastiness, assiduous, zealous, solicitous'; ba-g°äggu 'hastily, with haste, speed, assiduity'; g°agg°ä'at 'haste, speed'; 'ag°agg°ä'i, part. of 'ag°agg°ä'a.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (ta)ğāğāva (reduplicated ğ) 'flee'. Amh. g°ägg°ä 'hurry, rush', Gur. g°ägg°ä (reduplicative g°-g° or gy-gy, and probably be identified with g°yy 'flee', below). The comparison with Te. gäggə'a 'stutter (in a certain way)' suggested by Littman-Höfner 603 is unlikely. Te. gäggə'a goes back to g°-g° and is to be compared with Tna. gägg°ä bälä 'stutter'.

Amh. g°äläg°äl 'break up clods'.
