meaning: 'cover over', cp. Ar. ǧalāda 'cover with leather', PbH. ǧalād 'form a coating'. For this root see Wagner 40.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. ǧalādä 'hairless skin of animals', ǧild 'skin' (from Arabic). The meaning of 'skin' developed into 'belt with which one girds oneself'. Gaf. ǧalādä 'gird oneself', Gur. ǧalādä 'gird trousers, put on trousers'. Amharic (a)ǧalāddamā 'gird oneself with a loincloth called ǧalādəm' is a denominative of ǧaldəm, from ǧild with the suffix -m; Te. also ǧalād 'alliance, treaty', a secondary meaning of gild (see ǧaldät, above, 'skin' > 'girdle'. For the ǧildä 'having the meaning 'oath, treaty', cp. Gur. ǧurda 'oath', and Amh. ǧurda 'small belt' (see Leslau 1979:291). Amharic māǧarāǧa 'screen', from garrādä 'screen from view, curtain off' is not to be connected with G. ǧallada as suggested by Praetorius 1871:118.
ETHIOPIC: Te. ǧallafä 'well-dressed, be decorated, be adorned'.

meaning: 'knife with a curved point'; from Amh. ǧalādo; also in Te. ǧalōda, Tna. ǧalīdo; in Cushitic: Bil. ǧalūda, Sa.Af. ǧalōdä.
origin_explanation: From Amh. ǧalādo; also in Te. ǧalōda, Tna. ǧalīdo; in Cushitic: Bil. ǧalūda, Sa.Af. ǧalōdä.

meaning: 'grave, engrave, hew in stone, carve (a statue, an idol), sculpture, chisel, (Lt.K*) imprint, embroider, mold, form, fashion'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ǧalafa 'cut, scrape', PbH. ǧālap 'engrave', Aram.-Syr. ǧalap 'engrave', ǧelipa 'husked', Md. ǧlapa 'inset of a ring'. There seems to be no need to separate the Syriac root into glp I and glp II (Brockelmann 1928:119). Concerning Syr. glp 'carve' Krauss 178 rightly expresses doubts about the Greek origin of the root for 'engrave', as suggested by Rödiger and Brockelmann 1928:119. ETHIOPIC: Te. ǧallāfä 'engrave, carve', Tna. ǧalāfä, Amh. ǧallāfä 'make carvings, make idols'.
see ǧanfala, below.

meaning: 'assemble (intr.), come together, keep company, band together, gather in crowds, (Lt) be in an uproar, rage'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: G. angallaga is an abbreviated root from g-l-g-l and is to be compared with Gll. ǧigl (so also Dillmann 1141) with the meaning 'roll together > come together, be assembled'. For gll. ǧigl, cp. Heb. ǧālāl 'roll', ǧalgal 'wheel', PbH. ǧilgel 'roll', Aram. ǧalgal, Syr. ǧal (gll), ǧalgel, Md. ǧll, ǧlgl, Akk. ǧalālu 'roll' (von Soden 273). For more details on root, see D. Cohen 1976:118. The possibility of connecting gll with G. angargara (see below) expressed in Leslau 1958:15 is still a valid possibility. Botterweck 34 connects G. angallaga with the root gll (ǧll) 'be powerful, be strong' (Ar. ǧalla 'mass'). ETHIOPIC: Te. ǧälgäla 'gather', also in Cushitic: ǧalāl 'gather, bring together', Sa.Af. ǧal.

meaning: 'lay bare, empty, evacuate, separate, (T.Y.M) pillage, destroy'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Syr. galleg (from ǧlgl) 'lay bare' > 'break up' could develop into G. tagalgala 'go into captivity', it is also possible to identify the root ǧlgl with ǧly (see ǧalaya, below) > redupli- cated ǧl (that is, ǧlgl). For ǧly 'go into captivity', cp. (Dat) ǧalālä (ǧly) 'emigrate', Heb. ǧālā (ǧly), Syr. ǧālā (ǧly), Ug. ǧly. ETHIOPIC: Te. ǧälgäla 'tear off and split', Tna. ǧäl- ǧälä 'take out', Amh. ǧäläggälä 'uproot', G. ǧälä- ǧälä 'break up clods, pull out', ǧälālä 'make part', probably also ǧaläggälä 'separate two people who are coming to blows'. See also ǧll I.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. (a)ǧläggälä 'continue smoking', related to tg°lg°l 'in tātäg°läggälä 'billow upward (cloud of smoke)', tag°läg°äl 'cloud of vapor or smoke, emitting much smoke'.
transcription of Gk. gelgel, from Heb. galgal, nally 'sphere' > 'celestial sphere'; see also gerger below.

meaning: 'be visible, be conspicuous, be shaved (that is, be clear of hair)'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ǧaliḥa 'become bald', Heb. ǧālaḥ 'show, divulge (a secret)'. ETHIOPIC: Te. ǧälḥa 'be bald', ǧaluḥ (with ḥ) 'bald on the front of the head', Tna. ǧälḥayä 'shave the head when mourning'; related to ǧlḥ: Tna. ǧälḥe 'reveal', ǧälḥi, ǧälḥi 'visible', ǧuluḥ 'visible', ǧällä 'be visible, be conspicuous'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. ǧälḥay 'be bald', Som. ǧalaḥ 'bald head'.

meaning: 'engage in unseemly merriment, hit each other playfully'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. ǧälḥa 'engage in unseemly merriment, hit each other playfully'.
metathesis of gʷahlawa, above.

ETHIOPIC: undoubtedly to be identified with gʷalq°a 'as 'castor-bean plant' (Ricinus communis), gʷalq°a (in Reinisch 1887:149), Tna. gʷallag°laq°a; also in Cushitic: Bil. gʷulāʾa. Reinisch 1902:169 identifies Amh. gʷalq°a with Som. gulo, for which see gʷalo, above.

meaning: 'separate out, choose, set aside, move aside, winnow'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. ǧalālä 'move away from a place, make way', Amh. ǧalālä 'move away, part'; see ǧalālä, above.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. (tän)gallälä 'lie on the back'.

meaning: 'metal ornament surmounted by a cross placed atop a church'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. ǧullā 'horizontal projections on pillars' (Wajnberg 1937a:35), Akk. ǧullatu 'capital of a column'; Koehler 183 compares also Ug. ǧl 'cup, bowl'. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. gʷəllalat, same as Geez; gʷäl- lālā 'place the gʷəllalāt'.

meaning: 'give a plot of land (e.g. to a servant)'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. gʷəllāmā, same.
one recognizes gol, see above.

SEMITIC: Ar. ǧalama 'cut, slice off, prune', ǧalam 'scissors' (Dozy), Sh. ǧolum 'cut the top hair', PbH. ǧālam 'cut, separate'; related to Ar. ǧarama 'prune, remove the flesh from a bone'. ETHIOPIC: Te. ǧälma 'break off, cut into pieces', Tna. ǧälāmā 'take a bite of bread, break the edge of a jar', Amh. ǧälämmämä 'test something by biting it'.
from Amh. ǧalāmota, same; also in Tna. ǧalāmota.
for the ending -tu, see also zanzariyu.
from Gk. khamaileōn 'chameleon'. See also kamele- won.
From Gk. galēn (acc. sg. of galē 'weasel'); see gālē, above.

phonetics: galāwī (K*), act. part. of galawa; galāwī (Ll), pass. part. of galawa
meaning: 'also 'inlaid'; galāwī 'covering, cover, envelope, veil, darkness'; maglāw (K*), same as galāwī
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to gll (gll), gwl (gwl) 'horse blanket, blanket' (considered by Fraenkel 103-4 an Aramaic loanword, contrary to Schulthess 13), Ar. (Dat) gallal 'to cover', Ar. (Dat) gawwal 'cover the face', Ar. gilwī 'veil' (Dozy, (Fraenkel 216; for 'veil, sail' he compares Lat. vēlūm which has both meanings), Ar. galāwī 'wrapper, woolen cloak used at prayers'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. gālāwā 'cover a drum or a basket with leather'; Littmann-Höfner 563 compare Te. galot 'thicket'. The connection between G. gālāwā 'veil, cover over' and Soddo (Gurage) gubillā 'fog' suggested by Praetorius 1879:509 is unlikely. The transcription of the noun as gallā given by Brockelmann 1928:114 is misleading since it gives the impression that it is from gll, but in fact it is from the root glw. As for G. galawa, it is not to be compared with Semitic glw, gly 'uncover' as suggested by Dillmann 1140, Brockelmann 1928:115, and Blachère 1660. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Sa. gulā 'blanket', gōlo 'mat serving as a curtain', Af. gōli.
ETHIOPIC: Te. gālsa 'cover with hide', Tna. gālāsā, Tna.Amh.Har. galās 'saddle cover', Te. golas 'cover, wrapper', Gur. golas 'saddle blanket'.

SEMITIC: related to Ar. galata 'skin an animal, separate (an animal from the herd)'. ETHIOPIC: Te. gālča 'uncover, show', Tna. gālāsā 'disclose', Amh.Gur. Gālātā, Arg. Gālātā, Har. gālātā; passed into Cushitic: Bil. gallay 'disclose, show'.

meaning: 'bequeath, make a legacy, grant land, grant a fief'; tag°allata, passive; g°allātī 'legator'; g°alt 'feudal benefice, land grant, fief, legacy, patrimony, charitable institution, charitable legacy'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. gwl 'with full ownership rights' (Conti Rossini 1931:121). ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. g°allātā 'make a legacy, grant a fief'; Tna. g°əltī 'fief', Amh. g°əlt, Te. gəlt.

meaning: 'bodyguards, body of troops, commander of a thousand'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: perhaps Tna. gila, gilawa 'servant'; in this case -āt of G. gālāt would be a plural marker. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Sa. gilāwo 'soldiers, servants'.
probably to be identified with g°alāt, pl. g°alāt, for which see above.

meaning: 'cover over with leather, envelop, veil, wrap around, darken, obscure, prevent from seeing'; tagalwa, passive

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. galā (gly) 'uncover, unveil, make clear, emigrate' (note that Ar. ta-gallā 'reveal oneself, be bright [oncoming day]' seems to have been formed under the influence of Syr. 'elgā Jeffery 91), Heb. gālā (gly) 'uncover, emigrate', Aram.Syr. galā (gly) 'uncover, depart', 'leave', Md. gla (gly) 'uncover, exile'. Akk. galū 'remove impurities from a surface', Gur. galāwā 'separate'; also in Cushitic: g°ālay 'separate', Qua. gālē. See also galgala, galya. ETHIOPIC: Amh. galyāt 'lateral margin of a page' (from Geez). The root is related to gll: Amh. gāllālā 'remove impurities from a face', Gur. gāllālā 'separate'; also in Cushitic: g°ālay 'separate', Qua. gālē. See also galgala, galya.
from Byzantine Gk. galea 'galley' (Müller).
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Te. gamma 'crown, diadem' (Littmann-Höfner 566), Tna.Amh. gamma 'ribbon around the forehead on the occasion of festivities'. See also gāmā.

meaning: 'straight tree that is used in making carding bows'
origin_explanation: from Amh. gamme, same.

SEMITIC: related to Ar. gaym 'cloud' (Praetorius 1879:67), Syr. 'aymā 'mist, fog, cloud'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. gime, gāmā 'cloud, mist', Te. gim 'fog, cloud', Amh. gum 'fog, mist', gime (from Geez), Gur. gūwā (with w coming from Leslau 1979:XXXII); also in Cushitic: Sid. gomi-ččo 'fog', Aw. gum, Wol. gumā 'cloud', Bed. gim, gēm.

meaning: element -m, as is the case in Ug. g 'voice', gm 'aloud'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. gāmāgāmā, gāmā 'crash (thunder), rumble (thunder)', Tna. gəmyā 'exclamation used when one hears thunder for the first time'.
reconstructed from Amh. gāmma.

SEMITIC: related to gmm (see gamama, below). ETHIOPIC: Te. (tə)gammā 'break apart', Tna. gāmme 'split, separate', Amh. g°āmāgg°āmā 'appear, sprout' (reduplicative root of g°m > g°m) from the basic meaning 'break apart', also Gur. (a)g°āmā (from g°m°) 'be about to burst'. See also gumā, gammu.
probably originally a container from which one drinks; SEMITIC: PBh. gāmā 'drink, sip', Ar. gamā 'related to Heb. gm° (pi°el) 'swallow', (hif°il) 'give to drink'.
transcription of Heb. gome°(t). For the Egyptian origin of the Heb. gome°(t), see T. Lambdin, JAOS 73 (1953).149.
from Cushitic (Or. gumā, Som. guno, also Ka. gumō) through Amh. gumma; also in Har. gumma.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. gumma 'chunk of meat', from gam ma°a 'cut' (see above).
SEMITIC: Heb. gam in gam bākinū (Ps. 137:1) 'aloud we wept' (see Dahood, CBQ 22[1960].402). Heb. gam is composed of g with the adverbial element -m.
for gamb see ganaba, below.
from Amh. gāmād, same; also Tna. gāmād.

meaning: 'cut down, cut in pieces, cut up, amputate, decapitate, prune, harvest, (M) destroy';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ğamada 'cut', Soq. 'egmed 'be missing', Aram.-Syr. ğamad 'cut off, mutilate'; related to gdm (with metathesis), gdm (augmented by r); see gdm, above. ETHIOPIC: Tna. gəmədä 'cut, cut up', Te. gämd 'piece of cloth'. Amh. gämmädä, g'ämmädä 'cut'; augmented by r in Tna. g'ärmädä, Amh. g'ärmädä 'måddä; augmented by l in Amh. Gämäddälä 'cut, cut up', Gur. gəmäddälä; also in Cushitic: Qem. gomad 'cut', Sa. gamad.

SEMITIC: Ar. ğamma'a 'cut off short', PBH. gmm, Syr. gmm (gmm)'cut', Aram. gəmam 'cut, shave', Md. gumma 'diminishment', Akk. gəmāmu 'cut off'. ETHIOPIC: Te. gämamä 'cut off, clip', Tna. g'amä-mäsä; Tna.Amh. gämmämä 'shave the hair', Gut. gämämä 'chip the rims of a utensil'.

meaning: 'pollute, defile, profane, sully, soil, stain, contaminate, violate';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: de Moor 199 compares Ug. gmn which he translates 'defiled' on the basis of the Geez root, but see gmn II. ETHIOPIC: Amh. gämmänä 'commit a sin', (a)gäm-männä 'defile, pollute', related to Tna. gäbän 'wrong, defect, fault, blemish'.
SEMITIC: Ug. gmn is interpreted as 'sacrifice' (see Gordon 380 and Caquot 254), but see also gmn I. ETHIOPIC: Amh. gämmänä 'be burned, be scorched'; also gäbän (with alternance of labials).
ETHIOPIC: Tna. gumguma 'kind of bird'; also in Cushitic: Bil. gumguma.
ETHIOPIC: Te. gämha 'curtail (horns of a bull)'; related to gammə'a.

SEMITIC: Ar. ğamal, Soq. gəmal, Heb. gāmāl, Aram.-Syr. gamlā, Md. gumla, Akk. gammalu. For Gk. kamēlos 'camel' from Semitic, see Masson 66; for the root in Indo-European, see Buck 189. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. gämäl, Amh. Gur. gämäl, Arg. gamela, Har. gämila, gämila. While Kam. (Cushitic) gämela is taken from Semitic, the common Ethiopic and Cushitic root has the form gäla in Cushitic: Sid.Dar.Or. gäla (see Cerulli 1938:203 under gäla). Galla gäla passed into Zway (Gur.) gäla.
From Amh. gämär, same.

meaning: 'accomplish, complete, perfect, consummate, bring to an end, contain, compre-hend, include completely, hold, encompass, comprise';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ğamara 'collect, retain', Heb. 'bring to an end', Aram.-Syr. gəmar 'complete', Md.Ug. gmr 'be complete', Akk. gəmāru 'bring to an end'. ETHIOPIC: Te. gämmärä 'einsäumen, einfassen', to be translated 'enclose' rather than 'edge' (Littmann-Höfner 568), gämmärtay 'who takes a hand'. The connection with gmr 'begin' (Tna.Amh.Arg.Gur) suggested by Ullendorff 1955:71 is unlikely.
ETHIOPIC: Te. gämrot 'acacia', Tna. gomrat, Amh. gumäro, g'əmäro 'kind of strong vine', Gur. gəmar 'kind of tree', Har. gumär 'kind of from Cushitic: Som. gumar, Or. gumarä, gamäro, Kham. gimərä.

From Amharic. In the other Ethiopian languages Tna. gumarre, Har. gumärre, Gur. g'əmarre; from Cushitic: Qab. gumarru, Sa. gümäre, Som. gomäri. For the root in other Cushitic languages, see Cerulli 1951:444 under gomänö.
transcription of Gk. gomor going back to Heb. 'omer 'a measure of grain'; also in Amh. gomor (from Geez), Syr. gümür. See also homer.

meaning: 'ring for the little finger made of buffalo horn > small coin, penny';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. gamus 'ring made of hoofs of cattle' undoubtedly from gämus 'buffalo'.

meaning: 'remove the hair', gəmsa 'shorn of its plants, bare (expanse)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Te. gämsa 'tear off, bite off, cut off', Tna.Gur.Gaf. gämsa 'split, cut in two', Amh. gäm-mäsä; Tna.Amh. gəmsa 'half, half of a loaf of bread', Gur. guns 'bread'.

from Ar. gāmūs 'buffalo'; also Te. gamus 'European cattle without a hump', Amh. gāmus (from Geez); Cushitic: Bed. gāmus, gāmis. Spitaler 1955:215 in-terprets Ar. gāmūs as a secondary singular of *gäwā-mis from Persian gāw-mēš, lit. 'ram' or through Syriac gaumēš, gūmīše, gūmīsā, (also Md. gamūšam). Hommel 377 recognized that G. gāmus is represent-ed in Amharic as goš; cp. also Arg. goš, Har. goš, Gur. gäš; in Cushitic: Kham. guša, Sid. goše. Cerulli 1938:75 thinks that the origin of Cushitic *goš is Ar. gāmūs which became adapted to the Cushitic pattern. There is also the possibility of a Cushitic *goš coming from Ar. gāmūs (as suggested by Cerulli) and of the form goš being borrowed by Semitic-Ethiopic. Brockelmann 1950:17 states that Semitic-Ethiopic and Cushitic goš "ist das einhei-mische Wort mit dem Ar. gāmūs kontaminiert".

meaning: Amh. fila 'plant that grows on the border of the lake, papyrus';
origin_explanation: there is perhaps a connection with gome 'papyrus, reed' (for which see above).
a variant of gəbsas (see above) with alternance of labials b:m.

meaning: 'incline, bend, be bent, bow down, pervert (justice), be partial (in justice), pass a wrong judgment';
origin_explanation: Dillmann 1150 and M 504 make a distinction between gamasa and gammaṣa, but in fact the meaning is the same.

meaning: 'crooked, perverted', Tna. gamamasa 'tell a lie', Amh. gamamasa 'speak ill (of an absent person)'.

'gnaw, nibble, tear off a chunk with the teeth, (TS) diminish, (K*) calumniate, blame'; (T) renders it also by Amh. allaqa 'come to an end, but it should be asallaqa 'bring to an end, finish up'; Te. gamasa 'pinch lightly', gamat 'beater'; perhaps also gamasa (not gamita as in Leslau 1979:279) 'make poor' coming from the basic meaning 'nibble off, snatch away', Amh.Gur. gamamasa 'tear off a chunk with the teeth, take a bite, break the edge of a jar', Arg. gamamasa, Gur. gamata; Te. gahamasa 'tear into pieces' is considered by D. Cohen 1976:113 (under ghms) a cross between gmj and ghs; Te. gamahasa can likewise be interpreted in the same way.
'cut, (K*) estimate, evaluate'; gamata (Lt.M) 'quantity of a crop paid by a farmer to the landlord as rent, (T) piece'; ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh.Gur. gamamasa 'estimate, evaluate', Arg. gemmata.

'stick, (K) kind of snake'; there seems to be some confusion with the interpretation of this root. While T and Sw. in D have 'stick', K has gomit or gomit ganna 'kind of snake', gomit ganna meaning (as is the case in Amharic) 'poisonous snake about the length of a ganna-hockey stick', and one wonders why T and Sw. in D give only the meaning of 'stick'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. kama (kmy) 'hide'.
from Gk. genna 'birth, nativity of Christ'; also in Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. Har. ganna, genna; passed into Cushitic: Or. genna.

meaning: 'thresh, (L.T) heap up'; the meaning of 'thresh' seems to be a wrong interpretation of the sentence in 2 Esdras 23:15 quoted by Dillmann 1177 under gana 'a where yag'anna'u could mean 'they heap up'. As for g'anwa 'heap up', one has the impression that it is a misprint of g'ana (for which see below).

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. gana 'big jug', Tna. ganvi, Amh. gan, Gaf. gana, Har. gan; from Cushitic: Or. gani, Sid. gane, Qem.Sa. gan. Leslau 1958:9 and 1969:43 compares this root, with reservations, with Heb. gan 'bowl'. For the root gn, gn in the various Semitic languages, see D. Cohen 1970:7, Conti 1978:60, n.3. For the root in Hamito-Semitic, see Calice, no. 511, Cohen 1947:78, no. 12.

meaning: 'build a wall'; taganba (K*), passive; gamb (Lt) 'castle' (from ganb); ETHIOPIC: Amh. ganabba 'build a wall', gamb 'wall constructed of stones and mortar, (stone) palace'. Tna. gambi 'palace'; also Bil.Qua. gimb 'tower', Kam. gimbe 'make a wall'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: possibly Ar. gubba 'vessel of skin' (Dillmann 1176).

meaning: 'skin, hide'; Tna. ganobat 'skin, hide', Tna. gambo, gambo 'vessel for beer', Amh. gambo, gambo 'clay vessel', Gur. gambo; from Cushitic: Kam. gamba-ta, Qem. gambo.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh.Gur. ganbot; also in Cushitic: Qab.Tem. gambita, Kam. gambota. The derivation of ganbot from Ar. ganaba 'keep away', ganbot meaning 'month of abstinence' suggested by Conti Rossini 1925:495 is unlikely. Cohen 1931:382 takes as the basis of this noun the Caha form garm'at and derives it from grm 'be admirable'. However, garm'at is a normal phonetic development of ganbot with n and the alternance b:m.
from Amh. g'annada 'live alone'.

meaning: 'log, trunk, stem of a tree, stalk, log with openings through which a prisoner's legs are placed, lower part of an object, the foot (of a mountain), race, lineage'; g'and (T.K*) 'become like a trunk, become stocky' (denominative); g'and 'that became like a trunk, changed into a trunk, stocky'; g'andan (Lt) 'relatives'; for the meaning 'trunk, race, lineage', cp. PbH. geza 'trunk, race, lineage'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sh. gendet 'trunk'. The comparison Heb. gomed 'linear measure' (not 'stick') suggested by Brockelmann 1908:163), Syr. garmida 'ell, cubit' by Brockelmann 1928:133 and Koehler 188 is unlikely since G. g'and means 'trunk', not likely that a trunk should be used as a measure. It is more tempting to derive it from gamada 'cut down', gamd 'cut piece' becoming g'and, as suggested by Dillmann 1180 (and followed by Praetorius 1894:342). ETHIOPIC: Tna. g'andi 'trunk', Te. ganday, Arg.Gur. g'and; passed into Cushitic: Qem.Qua. g'and 'log', Sa. gund-e.

meaning: 'spider, place where there is water' (on the authority of Juste d'Urbin); perhaps rather a misinterpretation meaning such as 'gundan-spider that is found where there is water'; one wonders whether gundan is not confused with g'andan 'ant' given in K. As for 'ant', cp. g'andan, g'ande, Gur. g'anda; from Cushitic: gonda 'ant', Kam.Tem. gonda.
'shackle, fetter, chain, (Gr.338) yoke'; ETHIOPIC: Amh. gandur, gandor 'leg-irons'.
'who beats the drum'; ETHIOPIC: Amh. gandorahi. For the ending -hi as well as for the meaning, see also gabronahi.

'delay (intr.), tarry, linger, loiter, wait, be slow (in coming), be late in doing something (e.g. g'andaya hawira 'he was late in going'), last, remain a long time'; 'delay (tr.), delay, cause to delay, make slow in coming, put off, hold back, prolong, make wait, wait a long time, procrastinate'; 'be late'; 'loiter, be late, last long, delay, be of long duration'; 'think that one has come too late'; also same as 'delay'; 'after a time'; 'delay' (n); 'of long duration, delaying, delayed, slow, late, lasting'; 'since a long time ago, after a long time'; 'a long time, for a long time'; 'slow to anger'; 'who delays'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. dang'aya 'delay' (with metathesis), Te. gande 'stay a long time'. The derivation from g'and 'trunk' suggested by Dillmann 1180 (followed by Stade 42) 'stammig, dauerhaft sein' is not convincing. See also g'antaya.

meaning: 'wash clothes'; gannaf, gannafi, (OT 588) ganof 'fuller, who washes clothes'; ETHIOPIC: Tna. gannafa 'wash a clay vessel', Amh. gannafa 'clean a container by putting hot ashes and water in it'. Dillmann 1182 derives G. gannafi from Gk. gnapheus 'fuller'.
'carve, shape wood'; ETHIOPIC: Amh. g'annafa, same.
'porridge'; Kafa Gaf. Moa. ganfo 'grain boiled in water' are Amharic loanwords.
origin_explanation: from Amh. ganfo; also in Arg. ganfo, Gaf.Gur. ganfo; from Cushitic: Or. ganfo.

meaning: 'make bricks, (T.Y) make a wall'; ganfal 'brick', baked clay, tile, powder from bricks'.

'behave impetuously, be have arrogantly, get angry, speak vehemently, reprimand, (Gr.337) offend'; 'agnāha (K*), causative, (Gr.337) 'offend'; ganāhi (K*), act. part. of ganha; ganūh (K*), same as ganāhi; also 'who is offend ed'; ganāh (T) genāh 'vehemence, haughtiness, re buke, reprimand, scolding'; (qāla ganāh 'threatening shout'); ganāh (Lt) 'pride, haughtiness, violence'; 'agnāhi (K*), part. of 'agnāha'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ğunāh 'reproach, remonstrance' (but note that Ar. ğunāh is considered a Persian loan word [Jeffery 103]), PBh. ğānāh 'groan, moan', Aram.Syr. ğanāh, Akk. ganāhu 'have a fit of coughing'. See also g'ahana. ETHIOPIC: Tna. ganhe 'get angry, make re proaches'; perhaps Te. taganha 'show oneself > be proud > behave impetuously'.

meaning: 'heap up, accumu late, make a large amount'; (K*) = Amh. tāsākkāmā means in this context 'be filled up (ear of corn)'; tag"annak"a, passive; g"annāk"i (K*), act. part. of g"annak"a; g"ənnāk"at (Lt) 'act of piling up'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. g"annāk"a 'make a pile of mown hay or grain'. Reinisch 1887:221 connects it with Bil. K"ank"am, Qua. K"ank"a. See also g"an"a.

meaning: 'become impor tant, be proud'; yagnənā (K*), causative; gənūy 'notable, person of note';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. gənna (gən) 'exceed the measure', Amh.Gur. gännana 'increase, become important', Amh. gännana 'illustrious, important', Tna. gännana 'be numerous, be strong', passed into Cushitic: Qem. gänän 'obtain wealth'.

(za-gānən, pl. 'əllə gānən, 'əllə 'agānənənt 'de mon, lunatic, possessed by a demon'; gānən (Gr) 'demoniac, possessed by a gānənāwi, (K*) gānənāwy 'demon-pos sessed'. SEMITIC: Ar. ğinn 'demon' (see Noeldek Blachère 1791). A. Ambros is inclined to connect it with Syr. ğnn, (af-'el) 'aggen 'inhabit, res ing of a spirit'. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. ğan, ğinn 's from Arabic; Tna.Amh. ganen (Amh. ganen 'evil spirit', passed into Cu gānən. Qua. gānēn. For -el, -en in gānən kiruben, kirubel 'cherub'.
from Ar. ğānīn, same, in a dialect where ğ is pro nounced g.

meaning: (T) 'gather, bring together, put into a quiver'; mag"ənpa, məg"ənpa, (T) mog"ənpa, 'quiver, (T) cover for a spearhead';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Praetorius, ZDMG 47 (1893), 391 suggests a Cushitic origin: Som. gabayo 'quiver'. Grimme 1914:261 connects it with Ar. ğulbat 'Leder scheide für Amulette' (the existing dictionaries give 'amulet'), or ğilbāb 'wrapping'. The last compari son is unlikely since ğilbāb is borrowed from Geez and goes back to the root ğlbb (see galbaba), and the identification of gnp with ğlbb is not without difficulties. Dillmann 1182 connects G. mog"ənpa (qnb), miqnab 'bag, satchel'. Within Ethi opic the root gnp, g"np could rather be compared with Te. gānb-āt 'cover under a saddle', Amh. g"an-āt 'cover one's head', the basic meaning of the root being 'to cover, put into something', the 'cover' being either the 'cover for holding arrows, the cover under the saddle', or 'something to cover the head'.

meaning: 'distort, turn upside down, over turn, pervert, upset'; ganpala, passive; ganpāli (K*), part. of ganpala; ganpul (K*), pass. part. of ganpala; ganpalit, ganpalit 'perversion'; ganpalit-a 'the wrong way, perversely, re versed, inverted'; maganpal (K*), same as ganpāli;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the connection with Ar. G. qalaba 'turn upside down' suggested by Dillmann 1182 (followed by Grimme 1914:261, and Rūžička 134) is not convincing. ETHIOPIC: Amh. gänäbbälä 'overturn'.

meaning: 'be poor, be in distress'; genās (T.M) 'poverty, distress, privation, hard ship'.
'garden, the Garden of Eden, Paradise'; ('aqqābe gannat 'gardener'); (mədrə gannat 'Paradise');
origin_explanation: from Aram.-Syriac; also in Tna.Amh. gännāt; Har. gännāt, Gur. gännāt, from Ar. ğannat 'garden, orchard, Paradise'. As for Ar. ğannat, of Aram.-Syriac origin (Fraenkel 148-9, Noel de 1910:42, Jeffery 103-4). The root in the mean 'garden' occurs in SAr. gnt, Heb. gan, Aram. ginnə tā, Syr. gannətā, Pun. gnt, Ug. gn.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. gañña, geñña 'stallion', Gur. gañña from Cushitic: Or. gañña, Kam. gāña, Sid. gaña. Som. genyo.
from Sanskrit ghaṇṭā 'bell, gong'; also Amh. gäntä, Som. gändo. This noun is not to be connected with Amh. qänd 'horn'. G. qärn, as suggested by Prae rius 1879:78.
perhaps from Amh. gänäṭṭäṭä 'speak loudly'. As for gntt, could it come from the Ethiopic root gnh 'speak vehemently' (see ganha, above), noun *ganhat > gnt, with reduplicated last radical gntt?

meaning: 'make idols, serve idols'; gannāwi (pl. gannawt) 'idol-priest'; gənnāwə (T+) 'Holy Spirit';
origin_explanation: either denominative of gānən 'ghost, evil spirit' (see above), or possibly from ganaya 'render than serve' (so also Dillmann 1177). Noeldek 1910: does not think that gannawa is connected with gānən and suggests, with reservations, Syr. gənyā 'secret places for worshiping idols'. It comes close to Akk. ginū 'regular sacrifice', Md. ginia 'offerings to idols', but Akk ginū is considered a Sumerian loanword.

meaning: 'bow down, be submissive, thank, give thanks, render humble thanks, praise, do homage, serve, humble one self, acknowledge humbly, attest, beg, pros trate oneself'; 'agnaya, causative; also 'subjugate, subdue, subject'; taganaya 'be humble, ask humbly'; tagānaya 'humble oneself, be humble, worship humbly, entreat, beseech, praise, give praise, pay homage, laud, submit oneself, supplicate, make supplications, acknowledge humbly, confess sins, ask forgiveness'; 'astagānaya (K*), caus. of tagānaya; ganāyi (K*), act. part. of ganaya; gənuy 'submissive, who humbles himself'; gənāy 'homage, praise, thanksgiving, thanks, giving of thanks in humility, acknowledg ment'; gənyāt 'submission'; məgnāy (Gr) 'place of supplication or of paying homage'; taganyo 'thanksgiving'; tagānayət 'entreaty, supplication';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Aram.-Syr. gnā (gny) 'lie down, recline', Md. gna, related to Ar. ğanawa 'bend over some thing' (Dillmann 1178; Noeldek, ZDMG 54[1900], 158; Brockelmann, ZS 8[1932].115), and to Soq. 'i gnin 'bend, bow down'. ETHIOPIC: Praetorius 1879:7 connects the root gny 'find, meet' (Tna. 'agñaya, Amh.Gur. agäñña, Arg. agäñña, Har. agäña) with the Geez root gny. This identification is not convincing. Furthermore the connection between G. ganaya and Har. gäna 'throw away' suggested by Littmann, ZDMG 75 (1921).24 is unlikely.
from Cushitic: Bil.Qua. gän 'run'.

meaning: 'shroud, wrap in linen, → prepare a body for burial'; 'agnaza, causative;
(mashafa ganzat 'Funeral Ritual'; for a bibliography of this text, see Strelcyn 1974:12, Hammerschmidt 1977:12)
'material used in preparing a body for burial (bed, boards, linen)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ganaza 'conduct a funeral service' (from Geez: see Noeldeke 1910:58), SAr. gnz 'funeral'; ETHIOPIC: Te. gānza 'shroud, wrap a corpse', Tna. gānzāza, Amh.Gur. gännāzä; passed into Cushitic: Bil. genādāt 'corpse', magnad 'shrouding material' (with the regular alternance z~d), Sa. ganaz 'bury'.

ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. gänzäb 'money, treasure', Gaf. gänzābä; passed into Cushitic: Bil. gän dab, Kham. ginzib. Dillmann 1178 seems to have misread Aram. gənūzkā for ganuzbā, and consequently his question of an Aramaic loanword is unlikely. Praetorius 1890:41 derives ganzab from *gzb with inserted n and connects the root gzb with Ar. kasaba 'gain, acquire profit', ks > gz because of the voiced b. Brockelmann, ZS 5 (1927), 29 compares ganzab with Ar. kanz 'treasure', and he states that the b of ganzab is due to 'Reimwortbildung' with Amh. zgb in mäzgāb 'treasure'. In this case he would consider Geez ganzab to be an Amharic loanword.

FROM Gk. gyps 'vulture', gen. gypos, acc. gypa. T(ayyā), p. 183 registers three different translations: gipā = Amh. amora 'bird of prey', and in the next two lines: gipā = Amh. agāda, and gipā = Amh. sānbār, but it fact it should be gipā = agāda sānbār amora (for agāda sābār) 'bird of prey which breaks the leg bone'. Note that there is also a bird in Amharic called qältäm sābār 'bird of prey which cracks open bones to get the marrow'.
SEMITIC: Conti 1980:63, n. 3 compares, with reservations. Ug. grgr-t 'a weapon'; however, the meaning of Ug. grgr-t is doubtful (Gordon 381, Cag. ETHIOPIC: Amh. gera, gira 'metal helmet, diadem'.
(K quotes Ez. 45:12, but the Asmara Bible edition has 'aboli 'small coin'); transcription of Heb. gerā 'small coin'; Am. gera. In other Semitic languages: Aram. Akk. girū '1/24 of a shekel'.
maʔār-a gorā 'beeswax', lit. 'sweetness of the honeycomb'; ETHIOPIC: Amh. gāre 'hive from which honey has been taken'; probably also Tna. garna 'honeycomb' (from gar with the suffix -na).
probably reconstructed from Amh. gʷərrara.

gʷərʔarīt (pl. of gʷərʔe) 'the region of the neck' (see Dillmann 1907:313); gʷərʔaraya 'stab the throat, slaughter, strangle' (denominative); tagʷərʔaya 'kill oneself, (T) strangle oneself'; gʷərʔuy (K*) 'strangled'; gʷərʔərīt (K*) 'choking'; SEMITIC: the root occurs as grr, grr, and grgr (reduplicated gr): Ar. ğarara 'a swallow, gulp down', ğarğa ra 'gurgle', ğurğur 'noisy (throat)', Mh. ğorā 'drink', Heb. gārōn 'throat' (formed with the suffix -ōn), *gargarōt 'throat, gullet', PBh. gargerej, Syr. gagargartā (> gagargartā) 'throat', Md. gangarata, Akk. gaggaritu, gaggaritu, possibly 'gullet'. ETHIOPIC: Te. gara 'throat', Tna. gʷərʔara bälä 'gargle', gʷərʔaro, gʷərʔaro 'throat', gʷərʔarīt 'goiter', Amh.Arg. gʷərərro, gurorro 'throat', Gaf. gʷərara, Gur. gʷərārā. For 'slaughter', cp. also Har. gōrawa, Gur. gorā. The root is common with Cushitic: Sid. gorri 'slaughter', Kam. gora-ēččo, Som. gauara. See Cohen 1947:no. 206; Cerulli 1951:448 (under gʷər). For Indo-European gʷər for 'throat', see Buck 234.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. gʷərʔamā 'drink voraciously, drain off' in Geez the draining off refers to blood. Wajnberg 1935:11 derives Tna. gʷərʔamā, with reservations, from G. gʷərʔe 'throat'. See also gʷəryama.

meaning: 'leather bag, leather bottle, vessel of skin';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ğirāb 'bag' (see Fraenkel 64, 81), Soq. girib 'basket', Syr. garābā, Aram. garābā, Akk. girāb. ETHIOPIC: Te. garab 'receptacle, vessel' (possibly from Ar. ğirāb); also Amh. girba 'container (for water or honey) made of calf skin'; passed into Cushitic: Sa. girbo, Af. girib.

Since the Ar. text has gayhab 'male ostrich' (see Conti Rossini, RSE 10[1951],19, n. 9) one wonders whether G. garāb might not be a wrong transcription of Ar. gayhab. Getatchew suggests, with reservations, a wrong transcription of Ar. ğirāb 'crow'.
ETHIOPIC: Te. garbāb 'garment made of leather', Tna. garbāb 'linen cloth'.

SEMITIC: Ar. ğarada 'scrape, weed, peel', Heb. grd (hitpaʕel) 'scratch oneself', Aram.-Syr. gərad 'scrape off', Md. grida 'scraped', Ph. m-grd 'file'. ETHIOPIC: Te. gərd 'chaff', Tna. gurdi 'chaff', gʷərādä 'become chaff', Amh. gərd, gʷərdo 'chaff', gʷərādä 'separate chaff from grain'; also in Cushitic: Bil. gürd 'chaff', Sa. garad 'scratch off'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. Amh. garrādä, same.

meaning: 'be reduced' (used as a grammatical term, as in a verb final w or y such as talawa, the imperfect yətəlləw becoming yətəllu);
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. gʷərādä 'cut off, mutilate', Tna. gʷərdādä 'gnaw off', Amh. gʷərādä (gʷərdādä) 'cut off, be reduced'. See also gurdo.

meaning: 'small loaf of bread, kind of bread made from wheaten batter' (Amh. ləkāt, təbənənnə);
origin_explanation: to be derived from gorada 'be reduced'.
could it be from garād 'tribal chief (Har.), judge, governor' (Som.Sid), with an enigmatic ending -u or -li as in gardālī (below)? See also gazdəʔu.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Cushitic: Bed. gurda 'fever, disease'.

garif 'net'; SEMITIC: Ar. ğarīf 'trawling net'. Brockelmann 1928: 143 connects it with Ar. ğarāfa 'take away, sweep away', Heb. gārap 'sweep away' (so also Dillmann 1158), Aram.-Syr. garap 'carry away'.
məgəraf (K*) 'means of lashing'; ETHIOPIC: Tna. gəräfä 'whip', Te. gärfa, Amh. gürräfä, Arg. gürräfä, Gur. güräfä; passed into Cushitic: Kam. garraffo, Sid. garaf. See also gʷərifä.
probably a misprint for harif. Note, however, that KG likewise has garif.
from Amh. gʷərif.

meaning: (K) gʷarāfā ጐራፋ 'punishment, retribution, chastisement, torment'; from gʷarāfā II 'lash, whip'; cp. also Tna.Amh. gʷarāfīā 'punishment'.
origin_explanation: from gʷarāfā II 'lash, whip'; cp. also Tna.Amh. gʷarāfīā 'punishment'.
perhaps connected with gʷarīfā, above.

meaning: (L) gargel 'wheel, one of the seven heavens'; from gəlgl (by dissimilation); cp. Md. gargul 'wheel, sphere', Syr. gargālā. See also gelgel, gerger.
origin_explanation: from gəlgl (by dissimilation); cp. Md. gargul 'wheel, sphere', Syr. gargālā. See also gelgel, gerger.
See kirogarlīyos.

meaning: 'wallow, revolve, roll, roll oneself, make roll about, spin, drive round (tr., intr.), flop around, wriggle (one possessed by a spirit)'; 'angargārī (K*), act. participle; nagargār 'rolling, spinning, epilepsy, palsy, possession by a spirit, evil spirit'; (za-nagargār 'epileptic, one possessed by an evil spirit')'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ug. t-grgr 'bustle about, make haste'; to be identified with Semitic glgl, gəlgl (see glg, above) with alternance of liquids. ETHIOPIC: Te. 'angargāra 'roll, wallow', Tna. 'angargāra, Amh. nāgargār 'epilepsy'; also in Cushitic: Bil. egirgir 'move', engirgir 'epilepsy', Sa. gur 'roll', gargar 'roll', Som. girāngir 'wheel'.

meaning: 'murmur, mutter, grumble, complain, claim, be vexed, be angry'; 'angʷərgrārī (Gr.334), act. participle; nagʷərgr 'murmuring, whispering, complaint, indignation' (not nagʷərgr as in Dillmann 1158, but correctly nagʷərgr in the quoted passages); mangʷərgr 'who murmurs, who mutters, who complains, choleric'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: onomatopoetic, as in Ar. gargarā 'grunt, grumble'. For the connection of gʷərgr with Semitic gʷr (gʷr), ngr, see Conti 1980:62. Note that Ludolf records it under ngr; in 494 he compares it with Heb. rāgan 'grumble', metathesis in relation to ngr. ETHIOPIC: appears as gr, gʷr, gʷrgr, rgr, rgrgr. Tna. angʷərgrā 'mutter, mumble', Amh. angʷərgrā 'mutter', gʷərgr 'arr arā 'mutter', Gur. angʷərgrā 'grumble', (a)gʷrākʷārā (from agʷraggʷārā), (a)gʷərra 'roar', gur balā 'thunder', gurgur balā 'murmur', Har. gūr bāya, gurgur bāya 'thunder'.

meaning: (Lt) 'opponent, who opposes someone in a court case';
origin_explanation: from Amh. gārgārī, from gārāggārā 'be an opponent, oppose someone in a court case'.
variant of gelgel; see also gergel.
from Amh. gʷərgʷərtā.

meaning: (Cʷ) 'be mild-tempered, be meek, be gentle'; garh (K*) 'kind, meek, gentle'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. gārhe 'be kind', Te. gārha 'be good-natured', Amh. gārha.

meaning: (Cʷ) (K*) 'plough'; garāht (pl. garāhtāt, garāwāhī), garh (T) 'field, arable land, farm, estate';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 1153 (followed by Brockelmann 1908:169) compares it with Ar. qārāh, a root that has a variety of meanings in connection with land but refers mostly to land without vegetation (Lane 2511). However, the phonetic correspondence is not regular. ETHIOPIC: Te. gārhat 'field', Tna. garat (pl. gəraw) 'how'; also in Cushitic: Bed. garha.

meaning: (Cʷ), (KG) garh 'merchandise, freight, cargo, (T.Sw. in D 1153) money, capital used in commerce';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ǧarāha 'gain, acquire' (Dillmann 1153).

meaning: (Cʷ) (K*) 'tame, break a horse or mule'; garh 'tamed (horse, mule)'; garh, (T) garh 'tamed (horse, mule)'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. gārhe 'tame', Te. gārha 'be come tamed', Amh.Gur. gārra 'tame'. Arg. gārāha related to grr: G. 'agrara 'subdue', Te. gārāgāra 'get used to, learn', Tna. gārgārā.

meaning: (Cʷ) 'be frightful, be awesome, be fear-inspiring, be terrible, be formidable, become admirable, be amazed, also used impersonally with object suffix nouns: e.g. yəgʷarrəmomu 'they will be amazed'. garrama 'terrify, alarm, frighten, scare, frighten away'; 'agʷrama (Lt) 'make majestic'; tagʷarma, tagʷarama, tagʷarrama 'be terrible, fearful, be threatening, be terrifying'; gʷərum 'terrible, dreadful, terrifying, awful, fearful, wondrous, amazing, marvelous, majestic, august, venerable, tremendous, terrible thing, terror'; (gʷəruma gʷərumān [Lt] 'most terrifying') gʷarmā 'terror, fear, dread, awe, glorious, glory, majesty, dignity, magnificence, pomp'; gʷəramt 'terror, dread'; magʷramī 'terrible, terrifying, admirable, ordinary'; tagʷarmo 'intimidation'; təgʷramt 'terror, terrifying fear, threat, menace'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. gʷarāma 'frighten', Tna. gʷarāma 'astonish, amaze', Amh.Gaf. gʷarāmmā 'be amazing, derful'. Arg. gʷarrāma, Gur. gʷārāmā; passed into Cushitic: Sa. gʷaram 'terrible'. Qem. gʷārāmā 'surprise'. The derivation from Cushitic: Bish. rekʷ 'fear', Or. rogʷom 'tremble, fear' (rog- enlarged by -om), rgm becoming grm by metathesis, suggested by Praetorius, ZDMG 47 (1893).394, is unlikely.

meaning: (Cʷ) 'submit (intr.), be subject to, be subdued, obey'; garāra 'be subject to' (the intransitive is strange; to read perhaps wayagʷarrar [Synax. ary, gənbot 18] instead of wayagʷərrər; in this case the imperfect would come from garāra); 'agʷrara 'subdue, subject, submit (tr.), tame, make subject to, make submissive, overthrow, bend, be master of'; garārī, act. part. of garāra; gʷərur 'submissive, subject'; gʷərar 'submission'; gʷərat 'submission'; 'agʷrārī, same as magʷrārī; magʷrar (K.Gr.333) 'tamer'; magʷrarī 'subduer, conqueror, vanquisher'; təgʷrart (KG) 'submission'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: from grr 'pull, drag' > 'subjugate' (Dillmann 1155); Ar. garra, Heb. gārar, Syr. gar (grr). ETHIOPIC: Te. təgʷrārā 'be a vassal', Amh. gʷərrārā 'pull, drag' (from Geez), 'agʷrārā 'reduce to a tribute-paying status'.

meaning: (T.Y.Sw. in D 1156,M) 'build, make'; gʷərasa (T.Y.M) 'build, make snares to catch animals' (probably reconstructed from yəgʷərras, imperfect of *gʷərrāsa); gars (gʷars), gʷaris (pl. gʷarsāt, gʷarisāt) 'hinge, coupling, (T.Gr.439) chain, (OT.588) means of nailing'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. gʷərrāsā 'wedge (e.g. a loose peg in a hole with bits of wood so that it will be firm)'.

SEMITIC: Ar. ǧurn 'threshing floor, corn store', SAr. grn, Heb. goren, Ug. grn. Koehler 194, Baumgartner 195, and H. Rabin 1976:243 also compare Aram.Syr. gʷərnā 'large vessel'; add Akk. gʷarannu 'small vessel'. CUSHITIC: Bil. wārānā 'threshing floor', Kʷ. wārna (with gʷ becoming w; so also Reinisch 1887:360). On the other hand, Cushitic wrn could be compared with Te. wədna 'threshing floor' (with alternance d:r).
from Gk. grýppa (acc. of gryps 'griffin').

meaning: (K*) 'eat powdery or grainy food'; gʷaršā 'soft or powdery food, (Lt) a bite';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ǧarāša 'crush, grind (grain)', gʷarāša 'wheat coarsely ground', Heb. gārēš 'groats', Aram.-Syr. gʷəraš 'crush, grind'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. (a)gʷərrāšā 'make coarse-ground flour'; connected with Tna. gʷərrāšā 'take a mouthful, tear off food with the teeth', Amh. gʷərrāšā 'take a mouthful', Te. gʷarāš 'leavened porridge'.
ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. gʷarša 'a tree with edible fruit'; from Cushitic: Bil.Sa. gʷarša, Som. gʷaras.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. gärsa 'catfish-like fish'.

meaning: 'make an incision in the flesh in order to cup, (TS) bite'; magʷras 'means of cutting, means of scrap-ing, fangs, claws'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to qrs, qʷrs; see qaraṣa, qʷaraṣa. ETHIOPIC: Amh. garraṭa 'cut a vein in the forehead in order to bleed or cup a person', magʷarəṭ 'claws of a bird or animal'. Tna. ˀagʷräṣa 'prick, prod', gʷäräṣä 'abälä, gʷäräc̣ä 'abälä 'tear off with the teeth'. See also gʷaraṣa II, and qaraṣa.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. garrätä 'hold a prey in the mouth'. This root is possibly to be combined with gʷaraṣa I.
ETHIOPIC: Te.Amh.Gur. gərar 'acacia'; probably from Cushitic: Had. grära, Tem. grar. For gərar, see Streleyn 1973:no. 116.
from Ar. ğarra 'jar, vessel'.

meaning: 'draw blood with a suction cup'; gorem, pl. goräyam (K*) 'who draws blood'; magʷaryam (T.M) 'suction cup to draw blood'.
origin_explanation: see gʷarama.

SEMITIC: Ar. ğaraza, ğaraza 'tear, bite', Heb. gäraz, gäzar. ETHIOPIC: Te. gärza 'divide', Tna. gäräzä 'partition', gärzäwä, gärzäyä 'divide meat of a slaughtered cow', Amh. gärräzä 'circumcise', gäräzzäzä 'cut down a tree', Gaf. gäräzä 'cut', Har. gäräza 'plait (hair)', from the basic meaning 'separate'. See also ğaraza.
corruption of Gk. karpasinos 'consisting of karpatos 'cotton, linen'; also in Amh. gärzen 'silk cloth' (from Geez).

meaning: 'animal that is spotted, speckled, or has a black and white coat'.
'scrape away, shave off, (L) take away by force'.
origin_explanation: see gasasa II.
either from *gabs 'lime' (see gəbsas, above) > gasgas (reduplicated gas), or connected with Ar. ğass (see gəbsas) < *ğass (gass) > redupli-cated gs.

meaning: 'touch, feel, handle'; 'təgəsasa, causative; tagasasa, tagasasa 'be touched, be palpable, be tangible'; (zəyətəgassas 'palpable'); tagäsasa (Lt) 'slander, calumniate'; gasäsi 'who touches'; gəsus 'touched, handled, (Gr.478) withered'; gəssäsə 'touching, feeling' (n.); gassat 'touch (n.), touching, handling'; tagasäsi (Gr), part. of tagasasa.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ğassa 'touch, handle', Heb. gäsäs 'grope', Aram. gəšaṣ 'feel, touch', Syr.Md. gäṣ (gəšš). ETHIOPIC: Te. (tə)gasäsa 'honor by touching, kiss-ing, or prostrating oneself' (cross gəgäsa of Baum-gartner 198), Tna. (tä)gasäsä 'go around a church praying (and touching the walls)'. Amh. gäsasä 'feeling with the fingers' is taken from Geez. Note that tagasasa is a transferred meaning, for which cp. Amh. gässäsä 'degrade, humiliate'. See also gäṣṣ.

meaning: 'scrape away, shave off, pluck out (hair)'; tagasasa 'withdraw' (lit. 'be scraped off, that is, 'be put aside').
origin_explanation: See also gasgasa I.

meaning: 'wipe off with the hand, efface, wipe out', 'remove completely by cutting and digging'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ğaṭṭa 'tear out, uproot', Akk. gašäṣu 'cut down'. ETHIOPIC: Te. gäṣṣa (gsṣ), Gur. (a)-gäṣṣa; also in Cushitic: Bil.Sa. gäṣäṣ 'wipe', Kham. gis. See also gasgasa II.
perhaps from Lat. angustia 'of necessity' (Müller).
ETHIOPIC: Tna. gʷaṣäyä 'tend cattle'. Inasmuch as Abba Yaqob was Eritrean it is possible that he may have taken this root from Tigriña.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. gäṣṣi, Amh. gäṣṣ; probably from gasasa I 'handle' (see above).

meaning: 'belch, vomit, boil over, bubble, well up, utter, bring forth, speak (that is, belch out words), com-pose, (Gr.332) fly into a passion'; ugʷäṣa, causative; also 'instruct, express'; gʷäṣaṭ 'belching, bubbling'; (gʷäṣaṭä läbb 'the inspiration of the heart') magʷšä, magʷsä (T.M) 'belching, that which is ruminated'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. gʷaša 'belch', Ar. (Dat) tagʷašša, Hars. gəšəw (gšw), Syr.Md. gšy (gšy), Md. gšy, Gk. geušō, with metathesis in Heb. gä'aš 'retch' (J. Greenfield, HUCA 29[1958].205ff); related to gäṣṣ (gšy) 'belch'. See also Fronzaroli 1964:52. ETHIOPIC: Te. gʷäṣa 'belch', Tna. gʷäṣä, Amh. gʷäṣa, Arg. (a)gʷäṣa, Gur. (a)gʷäṣa, Har. gʷa-šä.

meaning: 'go on a rapid march'; 'ungäṣṣäṣa, (T) ˀangäṣṣäṣa 'agitate, be agi-tated, set in motion, excite, quiver, smite'; gäṣṣäṣä (Lt) 'rapid march'; nagäṣṣäṣa 'motion, commotion, agitation, pas-sion'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. gäṣṣa (gšš) 'become agitated, boil' (Lane 492), Akk. gäṣu 'whirl' (CAD 5:58), "etwa 'schnell laufen'" (von Soden 283). ETHIOPIC: Te. gäṣṣäṣa 'hasten along quickly, move quickly', Tna. gäṣṣäṣa 'be in a hurry', Amh. gäṣgäṣä 'move in a hurry, walk rapidly', Gur. gäṣgäṣä; passed into Cushitic: Kam. gasgaṣo, Qab. gasag-gaṣo.

meaning: 'admonish, chastise, reprove, rebuke, scold, exhort, urge, give an injunction, warn, discipline, instruct, teach, charge, correct, restrain'; tagäššaṣa, passive; also 'reform (intr.), take warning, be forewarned'; gaššaṣi, act. part. of gaššaṣa; gaššus, pass. part. of gaššaṣa; also 'learned, instructed, who has good manners'; gaššaṣe 'admonition, chastising, chastisement'; gaššaṣo (t) 'correction, exhortation'; magaššaṣ, part. of gaššaṣa; also 'teacher, re-former'; tagšaṣ 'rebuke, reproach, reproof, discipline, admonition, exhortation, correction, instruc-tion, chastening, chastisement, punishment'; (tagšaṣa salomon 'Proverbs'); (ba-tagšaṣ 'properly').
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. gäṣṣäṣä 'reproach', Amh. gäṣṣäṣä, Gäṣṣäṣä. Gur. gäṣṣäṣä.

meaning: 'face, countenance, aspect, appear-ance, presence, person (of Christ), each mem-ber of the Trinity, hypostasis, surface, direc-tion, side, front row (of army), cardinal point, front (of house), passage (of Scripture), page, point of view, chapter (of a book)'; (gasṣ bagasṣ 'face to face'); (gasṣa samäy 'firmament'); (habba gasṣu 'in detail'); (wäräqa gasṣu 'he went toward'); (qədma gasṣa + noun 'before a place'); (ˀarəˀa gasṣo 'he went toward'); (naˀa gasṣo 'have regard for, have respect for, show consideration'); gasṣawa 'represent, denote, (Gr.441) become a person' (denominative); ˀagasṣawa 'show, demonstrate, (T.Y) manifest, personify, describe'.

gassaw 'denoted, who has his face turned toward, directed (toward), evident, represented, personified. (Lt) steadfast'; gassāwə 'explanation, exposure, personhood, humanness, hypostasis, personality, demonstration, manifestation, synopsis, presentation, (Lt) steadfastness'; (śərrata gassāwə 'the lectionary for the year'); 'agassāwə 'personification'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: The connection with Sem. ḥzy (Syr.Heb) 'see' suggested by Dillmann 1208 and by Bittner, WZKM 23 (1909-10). 412 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. gāss 'face', Amh. gāss, gātt (from Geez). A possible denominative from gass is Tna. (ṭā)gəččāwā 'clash, collide' (that is, 'come face to face'). Amh.Gur. (ṭā)gəččā, Amh. (tān)gəččagāččā, Arg. (ag)gəčča, Har. (ṭā)gāčča; Amh. also gāččə alā 'appear in front of, to face', perhaps taken from Cushitic: Kham. ġas 'face'. Bed. ġedi, Bil.Qem. ġaz (see also Cohen 1947:no. 197).

meaning: 'dig a hole for planting, dig, scratch'; gʷəśfār (K*) 'itch';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. gʷəffāččārā (with metathesis) 'scrape, scratch'. See also gʷədfara, above.

meaning: 'corrode, erode, be rugged, be rough (road)'; gʷəśgʷəś 'as 'rugged, rough (road), rocky, knobby (wood), knotty, (T) hill, bank of earth';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. gʷəśgʷəś 'āṭṭä 'pry, dig out, scrape', gʷəśgʷəś 'āṭṭä 'uneven, rough'. Tna. gʷəśgʷəś 'as. See also gʷəś 'aṭ, gʷəś 'aṭ I.

meaning: 'examine, investigate'; (the form of the verb is unusual; it has the appearence of a denominative from gʷəś'ət-nat);

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: perhaps Amh. gʷəś'ətnat 'domination', gʷəś'əta 'master', the denominative verb meaning 'have the power to do things, such as examine, investigate'.

meaning: 'wear out (tr), make be out of use'; gəṭaṭṭa (K*) 'become old and worn-out'; gəṭaṭ 'worn-out basket, patched vessel, broken vessel, worthless vessel'; gəṭuṭ 'old basket, worn-out vessel, useless, sewn, patched, (Lt) spotted (cattle)';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. gāṭṭāṭä 'become old and worn-out (basket)', gəṭaṭčča 'old and decrepit, worn-out basket'. Tna. gəṭaṭ, passed into Cushitic: Or. gəṭīčča 'basket', Qem. gəṭačča.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. gʷəś 'aṭ 'knob, knot (of wood)' related to gʷəśgʷəś 'asa.
see gʷəś'aṭa I.
from Ar. gūṭ (pl. of ġā'iṭ) 'excrement', with the Arabic article 'al-, -əl-; see also gʷəś'ayasa II.

meaning: 'dig out (with a pointed object)'; gʷəś'aṭ, gʷəś 'aṭ 'point, pointed'; gʷəś'aṭaṭ 'tongs, pincers, (K*) fork';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. gʷəś'aṭaṭä 'scrape clean, cut the root', gʷəś'aṭaṭ 'āṭṭä 'dig out with a sharp pointed instrument'. Tna. gʷəś'aṭaṭä 'seize with pincers or tongs'. For gʷəś'aṭaṭ, cp. Amh. gʷəṭaṭ 'tongs, pincers'. Tna. gʷəṭaṭ; passed into Cushitic: Sa. gʷəṭaṭ. Dillmann 1208 compares gʷəś 'aṭ with gʷəś'aṭaṭa I). See also gʷəś'aṭaya.

meaning: 'dig out with a sharp pointed instrument, prick, (T.Y) fasten with spikes or nails, (K*) pinch, hold tightly'; gʷəś'aṭāyi (K*), act. participle; məgʷ'ṭay, məgʷṭay, (K*) məgʷ'ṭay 'ox goad, stimulus, peg, (T) spike';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. gʷəś'aṭayā 'prod with a stick', Amh. gʷəś'aṭaggʷ'aṭä, gʷəś'aṭaggʷ'aṭä; also in Cushitic: Sa. gʷəś'goad'. See also gʷəś'aṭaṭa I.

meaning: 'be thick (hair), be full (hair), have long hair'.
origin_explanation: perhaps related to gʷəś'aṭaṭa III 'become fat'.

meaning: 'cloth of red silk and gold thread';
origin_explanation: see also gʷagʷa.

meaning: 'dawn, grow light, break forth (light)'; tagʷəśhawa, (L) tagʷəśha 'dawn (v), begin to dawn'; gʷəśh, (T) gʷəśh 'dawn, daybreak';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) gawaḥ 'to dawn', gawh 'dawn', Heb. gāḥ (gawḥ) 'burst forth' (so Dillmann 1184; see also Noeldek e 1904:44); related to gʷəśhgah 'to dawn', and see gʷəśhəha, above. ETHIOPIC: Te. gʷəśha 'dawn', 'agʷśh 'early morning', Tna. gaḥ, gahgah 'dawn', Amh. goh, gʷəśh, Gur. balā 'to dawn', gʷəś'at 'dawn, early morning', the root is related to wġh: Te. wāġha 'break out (day)', Tna. wāġhe. Tigriña has also məgʷ'aḥat and bəgʷ'aḥ 'early morning'. Bassano 127 considers bəgʷ'aḥ "poco correttamente", but in fact the pronunciation bəgʷ'aḥat (for bə-gʷ'aḥat 'in the early morning') is correct one. Tigre has also fāngoḥ, fānguḥ 'to-morrow' from fān-goh/guh, from fāna 'toward' (attested only in Geez) and goh/guh 'morning, morrow' (for 'morning, tomorrow', cp. nagʷəś Rundgren 1955:222 analyzes fāngoḥ as *ba-nagʷəś > *banguḥ > fanguḥ; ib. p. 223 he suggests, with reservations, fānā-gih > fangoḥ without explaining fānā.

SEMITIC: Syr. gūḥā 'uproar, conflict', Md. guha. ETHIOPIC: Te. gāḥ 'crash' (n.), Amh. gʷəś'a alā 'crash'; also in Cushitic: Bil. gʷəś'ax y, gʷəś'ah y 'sound, ring'. Som. gūḥ 'noise'. See also gḥgh II.

meaning: 'dwell together in a neighborly way, live in the vicinity, be a neighbor, be near'; gʷəśgʷəś (pl. 'agʷār) 'neighbor, neighborhood, vicinity'; in Job 20:26 gʷəśgʷəś is used to translate 'stranger'; (ba-gʷəśgʷəś-a 'near');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ğār 'neighbor', Śh. ğər in Śəgər 'become a neighbor', Sar. ğər 'visit a sanctuary', Heb. gār 'dwell as client', gēr 'sojourner, newcomer', Syr. gīyūrā 'alien', Ug. gr 'foreign resident'. For 'stranger, enemy' and 'guest', see Blachere 1892-3, 1897 who compares Latin hospes 'stranger, guest', hostis 'stranger, enemy'; see also Baumgartner 177 (under gwr), 193 (under gər), and Müller 1962:39. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (ṭə)gawārā 'be a neighbor', Tna. (ṭə)gawārā; Te.Tna. gər 'neighbor', Amh.Tna. gʷəś'arābet (from gʷəś'a bet), Arg. gʷəś'arābed, Gaf. gʷəś'arābeṭā; passed into Cushitic: Sa. gər 'neighbor'.

meaning: name of a sign of the zodiac (Gemini);
origin_explanation: from Ar. gawzāś; see also gawzā.
from Ar. gawṣān 'coat of mail'.

from Aram.-Syr. gawzā (of Persian origin), as is also the case of Ar. gawz (Noeldeke 1910:43; Wagner 18). In the other Ethiopian languages: Tna. Amh. gʷaz, Tna. gāwzi. For Heb. 'əgoz, Aram. 'angūzā to be identified or not with gawz (with a prothetic ), see Noeldek e 1910:43, n. 5; Baumgartner 10; Wagner 18. For the Hittite origin of this root, see Rabin 1964:151. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil gawz 'nut'.

meaning: name of a sign of the zodiac (Gemini);
origin_explanation: from Ar. 'al-gawzāś; see also gaws.

meaning: 'kind of pea believed to cause paralysis of the legs';
origin_explanation: from Amh. gʷəś'ayyā; probably from Cushitic: Qem. Kham. gʷəś'ayā, Or. gayo.
from Amh. gʷəyəyā.
SEMITIC: Brockelmann 1908:233 connects it with Ar. gāwb 'basin'. ETHIOPIC: Te. gaybo 'vase', Tna. gʷəybi, Amh. geb 'tumbler', Har. gēb, also in Cushitic: Sa. gaybe.
from Ar. gayb.

SEMITIC: Ar. ġāda (gwd) 'be swift (horse)', Hars. ged (gyd) 'good, fine', SAr. gwd 'swift'. For the Arabic root, see Blachère 1879ff. ETHIOPIC: Te. geda (gyd) 'hurry', Amh. gāyəd 'spirited horse', Gur. gaday (with metathesis) 'fast, rapid, short-tempered'. Tna. gāyədā 'not walk well (horse)' is to be connected with Te. gedo 'horse (mule) that does not run well', and not with Te. geda as suggested by Littmann-Höfner 597.

meaning: 'hasten, hurry, be rapid, be fast, gallop'
'salt bar used as currency' (so called because it is a cut piece)
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. gāylā 'bar of salt' (called amole in Amharic); metathesis in relation to galaya, above.
from Ar. ġīr 'lime', of Aramaic origin (Noeldeke 1910:45; Wagner 42); also SAr. gyr 'plaster', Heb. gīr. For Ar. ġīr, see Dozy I 238, and Blachère 1974.

meaning: 'a kind of white soil', also from Arabic
origin_explanation: Te. gərgər (reduplicated gər) 'limestone' is undoubtedly to be connected with gīr.

meaning: 'be a stranger, be an alien, (H) emigrate'
origin_explanation: see gwr, tagāwara.

meaning: 'be early, do things early in the morning (such as rise, come, leave, go, travel)'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Modern South Arabian ghm, ġhm. Šh. gehem 'come in the morning', Mh. ġihem 'travel', is connected by Bittner 1914:25 and Rundgren 1955:254 with Ethiopic gesa, gesam-a 'morning' (the Modern South Arabian ghm would then be a denominative from gesam). While the alternance ġ/h is possible for Modern South Arabian (see Leslau 1938:33), such is not the case in the Arabic dialects of South Arabia where the root ġhm means 'leave in the morning' (Landberg 1920:304-5). For that matter, gahma 'end of the night' also occurs in Classical Arabic, and in SAr. ghm-y 'last part of night, just before dawn'. H. Bauer, ZDMG 69 (1915), 566 compares G. gesa with Ar. ġāsa 'bubble boil over, rush toward' > 'set out early'. ETHIOPIC: Te. gesa 'walk, travel (in general at any time of the day)', Tna. gesā 'travel to a foreign country', gis 'go away!' (used in a game). For morning, tomorrow', cp. Har. gīs 'tomorrow', Gur. gēs. Amh. magast 'the next day' (with the prefix ma- and the suffix -t). It is the same magas that occurs in maksānno 'Tuesday' for mags-ānno, from ma-gs-ānno (Word 3 [1949],277-8). Another expla-nation of maksānno is given by Rundgren 1955:253. Note that some meanings of gsgs coincide with those of the root gesa (gys); thus Tna. gāsaggāsā 'leave early in the morning'. Te. gāsaggāsā 'move along quickly'. Amh. gāsaggāsā, probably originally 'move on quickly in the morning'. For gesam 'tomorrow' (from gesa-m), cp. Te. gesām, and for ending -m, also tamālām 'yesterday'. For the end-ing in Semitic, see H. Torczyner, Die Entstehung des semitischen Sprachitys (1916),144. Cerulli 1936:251 thinks that the Semitic-Ethiopic root is borrowed from Cushitic: Bil. kes 'becoming', Qem. kās, kīs. Note that Bil. gažāy 'traveler' is taken from Te. gašay.

T. 115 also renders Geez gāyso by Amh. gʷʾālmā (gulmassa) 'growing to young manhood'. For that meaning there seems to be some connection with Amh. gāššā 'shield' meaning also 'military prowess'. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Arg.Gur. gaššā 'shield', Gaf. gašā. Guidi 772 also records Amh. gayso 'kind of spear'; from Cushitic: Sid. Qem.Qua. gāšā, S. gōlan (Cerulli 1951:437, under gaččo). Ludolf derives G. gāyso from Gk. gaisos 'javelin'. The derivation of Amh. gaššā from G. gəhšā 'ward off' suggested by Praetorius 1879:171 is unlikely.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps to be identified with Te. ma-gays 'way of life'.
gʷʾayas (T.M) 'excrement'; see gʷys, gʷəsa.

meaning: 'offend, be hostile, be an adversary, be an enemy, be opposed (to), plague, vex, quarrel, argue, disdain, scorn, be haughty, show arrogance, give oneself airs'; (note that K interprets tagāyasa as a passive); gīyās 'contention, strife'; tagāyaso (Lt) 'hostilities'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. (tə)gayyasa 'be hostile', Amh. (tā)gāyšā 'be defiant, be hostile, be obstinate' (in "Amharische Kaiserlieder" according to Littmann-Höfner 598); Amh. (tā)getā-bhai 'he derided him' comes either from a getā 'deride' (cp. Geez tagāyāsa) or from (a)getā 'he was dressed up in his best (against him) > he showed off (against him) > he derided (him)'. The basic meaning of the Geez root seems to be 'be hostile, vex' which developed secondarily into 'be haughty', and it would seem that the connection with gʷys 'decorate, adorn' (see gʷāyasa I) suggested by Praetorius 1879:146 is unlikely, unless the basic meaning is 'be haughty'. For the meaning 'be haughty', cp. Ar. ġāda 'strut' (D. Cohen 1976:107).
ETHIOPIC: Tna. gʷaysi 'decoration', Amh.Gur. (a)-gʷāyo 'adorn'. Arg. (a)geta. See also gʷāyasa I.
see gʷāyasa II.
see gʷəyasa I.

SEMITIC: Soq. ge 'flee, hurry'; related to Syr. gahā 'flee, escape'; Md. gha 'flee from'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. gʷəyāyā 'flee, run away', Amh. (ən)gʷəy 'who flees', Gaf. gʷəyā (for ġ < y, see Leslau 1956:9). See also gʷaggʷəʾa.
from Gk. gaza 'treasure'; also occurs in Aram.-Syr. gazzā going back to Median ganza (Wagner 41). As for Gk. gaza, see Wagner 41, Masson 30, n. 1. See also gizā.
refers perhaps to the city of Gaza in connection with the baptism in Acts 8 (Getatchew).
perhaps from Ar. gāzā(t) 'military expedition, attack'
perhaps a variant of gāz, gāzā, above, or from Ar. ğāz 'onyx'

meaning: 'time, a time, season, hour, moment, period'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. gize, Gaf. gəzye, Gur. ga-z(ə)yā. The derivation of gize from Ar. ğāza 'pass' as suggested by Dillmann 1196, and Praetorius 1879:173 is doubtful. Likewise the connection with the root gzw (gəzw) 'cut, determine, fix (time)' suggested by D. Cohen 1976:110 is doubtful. It is perhaps to be analyzed as gi-ze 'this time' (Rundgren 1955:44); for gi, cp. Gur. gi, ge, ga (Leslau 1979:254). The root also occurs in Cushitic: Qem. Dar. gizāta. Plazikowsky-Brauner, MIO 1/2 (1953):267 connects it also with Sa. gēd, gēdā.
SEMITIC: PBh. gaz 'bird of prey', Aram. gāzā. ETHIOPIC: Amh. g°əzā; the derivation from Kafa gačo suggested by Brockelmann 1950:26 is unlikely.
from Amh. g°ə'əzo.
SEMITIC: Ar. gazā 'assign', Sar. gz 'assign', Heb. gāzā 'assign', Syr. gāzā 'assign'.

SEMITIC: Dillmann 1191 connects this with gāzā (gzy) 'reward'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. gāz'a, gārz'a 'have a drinking bout'. Amh. gəzəwə (with metathesis) 'kind of banquet' passed into Cushitic: Bil. garda 'serve at a feast'. See also mgz.

SEMITIC: Ar. gazā 'cut', SAR. gz 'assign', Heb. gāzā 'cut', Syr. gāzā 'cut', gāzā 'cut'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. gāz'a 'divide, separate', Amh. gāzā 'saw, cut with difficulty', Amh.Arg.Gur. gāz 'saw', Tna. māgaz (from Amharic), and a nominative in Tna.Amh. māggāzā 'cut with a saw'.

meaning: 'person of high social or free status'
origin_explanation: perhaps a misprint for gardə'u (see above).

meaning: 'be thick, be dense, be stout, be obese, become fat'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. gāzfa 'be thick, be big', Tna. gāzfa, Amh. gāzzāfa.
from Amh. gāzzāfa.

meaning: 'put up a bed', gə'əzg'əz (pl. g°ə'əzg'əz, gə'əzg'əz) 'covering, spread, carpet, mat, mat made of rush'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. gə'əzg'əzā 'cover the floor with fresh grass or rushes', Amh. g°ə'əzg'əzg'əzā, Arg. g°ə'əzg'əzg'əzā, Har. guzəguz āsa; Amh. gəzəgəz 'fresh grass or reeds for spreading on the floor', Tna. guzəgəz 'mattress'.

meaning: 'cut, cut down, cut off, retrench, tear up, rend, hew (wood); despise, hold in contempt'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. gazama 'cut', Soq. məgzimoh 'ruin' (from gzm), Sar. gzm 'extirpate, defeat an enemy, conclude a pact', Heb. gəzam 'cut', Aram.-Syr. gəzam 'cut off', Md. gzm 'threaten, be violent'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. gəzāmā 'divide up meat of a slaughtered animal', Amh. gəzzāmā 'divide land into lots', also 'conquer, vanquish' (for which see SAR. gzm); Tna. gəzāmo 'big axe', Amh. gəzāmo, gāzāmo, Gur. gūnzāmā (augmented by n), Tna. gūzmi 'dowry' < 'pact'.
bəṭā gizān 'house of treasure'; see gāz.
SEMITIC: Ar. ğazara, ğazara 'cut, slaughter', Sh. gəzər, Heb. gāzar, gāraz, Aram.-Syr. gəzar 'circumcise', Ug. gzr 'cut' (Caquot). ETHIOPIC: Te. gānzāra 'cut meat into pieces' (from Amh. gāzāra).
from Ar. ğazīra; also Te. gəzīrät, Syr. gəzurtā.
see wgz. Note the denominative gzt (from gəzät) in Tna.Amh. gäzzätä 'excommunicate'.

meaning: (K*.Y.M) 'cut, split, cleave asunder, slaughter'; 'agzaza (K*), caus. of gəzaza; also 'diminish'; gəzāzi (K*), act. part. of gəzaza; gəzazä (M) 'cleft, crack, split';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. ğazza 'cut', Soq. gezz, SAr. gzz 'assign property rights' < 'cut' (Müller 1963: 308), Heb. gāzaz 'cut off', Aram. gəzaz 'cut', Syr. gaz (gzz), Md. gzz, Ug. gzz 'shear', Akk. gazāzu. ETHIOPIC: Te. gäzza (gzz) 'divide', Tna. g'äzäzä 'split', Amh. gäzäggäzä 'saw, cut', Gur. gəzägäzä 'cut a part of the body'; related to gzr (see gaz'a).

meaning: see 'agzaza; 'agzaza 'surround with a hedge, fence in, enclose, make a palisade'; magzaz, magzəz 'that which serves to enclose (wall, veil), means of screening (curtain)';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. gäzzäzä 'make a palisade or a fence'; perhaps connected with gzz I 'cut > separate > surround with something (e.g. hedge) that separates one section from another'.
